59	   (`--write-mapping <path>`).
60	
61	The temp SHA1 store is on disk, not in memory, so peak RAM is bounded
62	by the in-memory mapping plus a small fixed delta-resolution cache.
63	Large repos still work; expect runtime dominated by the network fetch
64	and the loose-object write throughput.
65	
66	## Handling External SHA1 References
67	
68	A SHA1 → SHA256 cutover is destructive for external systems that
69	reference commits by hash: PR descriptions, issue trackers, deploy
70	logs, container labels, doc links, and so on all stop resolving. The
71	command offers three on-ramps for migrating those out of band.
72	
73	### 1. Inline message rewriting (default on)
74	
75	Commit and tag messages are scanned for 7-to-40-character hex runs.
76	When a run uniquely matches the SHA1 of an object translated in the
77	same conversion, it is replaced with the full SHA256 hex. Examples
78	that get rewritten:
79	
80	```
81	Reverts: a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2a3b4c5d6e7f8a9b0    →    full SHA256
82	Cherry-picked from a1b2c3d                            →    full SHA256
83	```
84	
85	False positives are essentially impossible because a run is only
86	substituted if its prefix uniquely matches a real SHA1 in the
87	mapping. Disable with `--no-rewrite-messages` if you prefer untouched
88	messages.
89	
90	Limitation: a reference is only rewritten if the referenced commit was
91	already translated when the referencing commit is encoded. Ancestor
92	references always satisfy this (DFS handles parents first), but
93	cross-branch references — for example, a cherry-pick from a sibling
94	branch — depend on the order of ref iteration and may not resolve.
95	The origin notes ref and the mapping file (below) cover the gap.
96	
97	### 2. Origin notes ref (default on)
98	
99	`refs/notes/sha1-origin` is written after translation and holds, for
100	each translated commit, the pre-conversion SHA1 hex keyed by the new
101	SHA256 hash. Standard git tooling can read it:
102	
103	```bash
104	git -C /path/to/out.git notes --ref=sha1-origin show <sha256>
105	# prints the original SHA1
106	
107	git -C /path/to/out.git log --notes=sha1-origin
108	# shows the original SHA1 below each commit's body
109	```
110	
111	Notes attach meaningfully only to commits, so blobs, trees, and tags
112	are not represented in this ref. Disable with `--no-origin-notes`.
113	
114	### 3. Sidecar mapping file (opt in)
115	
116	`--write-mapping <path>` emits a TSV with one line per translated
117	object, sorted by SHA1:
118	
119	```
120	# sha1   sha256
121	00027b675386b21c4ca05316145671fb7034d251   d80415fa21bebb...
122	000bb155604d06f1c48fc7feb4b025d991ef3366   a23cf98db5abfa...
123	...
124	```
125	
126	Useful for bulk rewriting external systems: feed the file to a
127	script that walks Jira tickets, PR bodies, deploy manifests, or any
128	other system that holds frozen SHA1 references.
129	
130	## Flags
131	
132	```
133	--source-url                       source repository URL
134	--source-token                     source password/token (prefer env)
135	--source-username                  source basic auth username (default git)
136	--source-bearer-token              source bearer token
137	--source-insecure-skip-tls-verify  skip TLS verification (testing only)

input.old_string:
## What It Does

1. Probes the source via smart HTTP and discovers refs matching the
   requested scope (`--branch`, `--tags`, `--all-refs`, `--map`,
   `--exclude-ref-prefix`).
2. Fetches a single self-contained pack via `upload-pack` and lands it in
   a temporary on-disk SHA1 bare repo. The temp directory is cleaned up
   on exit unless `--keep-source-objects` is passed.
3. Initializes the target as a bare SHA256 repository
   (`git init --object-format=sha256` equivalent).
4. Walks every reachable object from each desired ref tip and re-emits
   it with rewritten hashes:
   - **Blobs**: re-hashed under SHA256; content unchanged.
   - **Trees**: each entry's hash translated via the in-memory mapping;
     submodule gitlinks left as-is when the referenced commit is in
     this repo, otherwise the run errors.
   - **Commits**: `tree` and `parent` hashes translated; GPG signatures
     dropped; `mergetag` extra headers dropped; commit message hashes
     rewritten (see below).
   - **Tags**: target hash translated; signatures dropped; tag message
     hashes rewritten.
5. Writes refs in the SHA256 target at the translated tip hashes. HEAD
   is repointed at the source's symbolic HEAD when that ref made it
   into the conversion.
6. Optionally writes the SHA1 → SHA256 mapping as a TSV sidecar
   (`--write-mapping <path>`).
input.new_string:
## What It Does

1. Probes the source via smart HTTP and discovers refs matching the
   requested scope (`--branch`, `--tags`, `--all-refs`, `--map`,
   `--exclude-ref-prefix`).
2. Fetches a single self-contained pack via `upload-pack` and lands it in
   a temporary on-disk SHA1 bare repo. The temp directory is cleaned up
   on exit unless `--keep-source-objects` is passed.
3. Initializes the target as a bare SHA256 repository
   (`git init --object-format=sha256` equivalent).
4. Runs a **discovery pass** that walks every reachable object from
   each desired ref tip and records its SHA1 and object type. This
   gives the rewriter an authoritative "what's in scope" set so
   abbreviated message references can be resolved consistently and
   message-reference edges can be added to the translation graph.
5. Translates every reachable object in topological order via a
   memoized DFS:
   - **Blobs**: re-hashed under SHA256; content unchanged.
   - **Trees**: each entry's hash translated via the in-memory mapping;
     submodule gitlinks left as-is when the referenced commit is in
     this repo, otherwise the run errors.
   - **Commits**: `tree` and `parent` hashes translated; GPG signatures
     dropped; `mergetag` extra headers dropped; in-scope SHA1
     references in the message are translated first (so their SHA256s
     are known) and then substituted into the message.
   - **Tags**: target hash translated; signatures dropped; tag message
     hashes rewritten with the same edge mechanism as commits.
6. Writes refs in the SHA256 target at the translated tip hashes. HEAD
   is repointed at the source's symbolic HEAD when that ref made it
   into the conversion.
7. Optionally writes the SHA1 → SHA256 mapping as a TSV sidecar
   (`--write-mapping <path>`).

Cherry-picked from a1b2c3d                            →    full SHA256

input.new_string:
### 1. Inline message rewriting (default on)

Commit and tag messages are scanned for 7-to-40-character hex runs.
When a run uniquely matches a commit or tag SHA1 in the conversion's
reachable set, it is replaced with the full SHA256 hex. Examples that
get rewritten:

```
Reverts: a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2a3b4c5d6e7f8a9b0    →    full SHA256
Cherry-picked from a1b2c3d                            →    full SHA256
```

Two design notes:

- **Uniqueness is decided against the reachable set, not the
  in-flight mapping.** The discovery pass enumerates every reachable
  SHA1 before any encoding starts, so abbreviated prefixes get the
  same verdict regardless of how far the translation has progressed.
  If `a1b2c3d` matches two different commits in scope, it is treated
  as ambiguous and left alone — never rewritten on the basis of which
  one happened to be translated first.
- **Cross-branch references work the same as ancestor references.**
  Each in-scope SHA1 mentioned in a commit's message is added as a
  dependency edge in the translation DFS — that commit is translated
  before the referencing commit is encoded. So a cherry-pick from a
  sibling branch resolves just as reliably as a revert of an
  ancestor. (Cycles in this graph are cryptographically impossible:
  for both A's message to contain B's SHA1 and vice versa, you would
  need to know each hash before computing it.)

False positives are essentially impossible because a run is only
substituted if its prefix uniquely matches a real SHA1 of a commit or
tag in scope. Blob and tree hashes are excluded from the match set
so incidental hex strings that collide with content hashes are not
rewritten. Disable with `--no-rewrite-messages` if you prefer
untouched messages.

content.text: Also update the "External SHA1 references break silently" section and the "Implementation Notes" section to reflect the new design:

result.output:
175	Environment fallbacks: `GITSYNC_SOURCE_TOKEN`, `GITSYNC_SOURCE_USERNAME`,
176	`GITSYNC_SOURCE_BEARER_TOKEN`, `GITSYNC_SOURCE_INSECURE_SKIP_TLS_VERIFY`,
177	`GITSYNC_SOURCE_FOLLOW_INFO_REFS_REDIRECT`, `GITSYNC_PROTOCOL`.
178	
179	## Sharp Edges
180	
181	**GPG signatures are stripped.** A signature is bytes signed over the
182	commit's pre-conversion content (including the SHA1 `tree` and
183	`parent` lines). After rewriting, the bytes no longer match the
184	signature, so verification would always fail. Rather than persist
185	invalid signatures, the command drops them and prints a warning count.
186	This matches upstream `git`'s own SHA256 conversion behavior. Tags
187	with embedded signatures and `mergetag` extra headers are handled the
188	same way.
189	
190	**Submodule gitlinks must resolve in-repo.** Tree entries with mode
191	`160000` reference a commit in another repository, but a SHA1 hash
192	cannot be embedded in a SHA256 tree. The command translates the
193	pointer if the referenced commit happens to live in the same store
194	(rare; sometimes seen in vendored modules), and otherwise exits with
195	an error naming the offending tree, entry, and hash. Scope around
196	those refs with `--exclude-ref-prefix` or `--branch`, or convert the
197	submodule repository first.
198	
199	**External SHA1 references break silently.** See the section above for
200	mitigations. Even with all three on, references in commit messages on
201	branches that weren't processed first, and any references outside the
202	repo, remain SHA1.
203	
204	**No hosting forge accepts SHA256 pushes today.** GitHub, GitLab.com,
205	and Bitbucket all advertise only SHA1. Self-hosted Gitea/Forgejo do
206	accept SHA256, as does plain `git http-backend` and `git daemon`. For
207	internal mirrors, serve the converted repo via your own server.
208	
209	**Replace refs and notes refs become detached.** `refs/replace/<sha1>`
210	encodes a SHA1 in the ref name itself, so the name doesn't match
211	under SHA256 and the replacement never triggers. `refs/notes/*` paths
212	encode the target object's hash as a tree path, so existing notes
213	copied under `--all-refs` survive as data but no longer attach to
214	their original commits. Neither is a correctness issue, just lost
215	behavior.
216	
217	**Reflog is not preserved.** The target is a fresh bare repo with no
218	operational history. Anyone relying on `git reflog` to find lost
219	states should snapshot before conversion.
220	
221	**HEAD can dangle.** When the source's symbolic HEAD branch is not in
222	the desired ref set, the target's HEAD is left at `refs/heads/master`
223	(go-git's PlainInit default) and resolves to nothing. Either include
224	the HEAD branch in scope or set it manually after conversion with
225	`git -C <target> symbolic-ref HEAD refs/heads/<branch>`.
226	
227	**Storage is all loose objects.** The command writes one file per
228	object. Correct, but on filesystems that dislike millions of small
229	files this is slow. Run `git -C <target> gc --aggressive` afterwards
230	to pack the converted repo down to a single packfile.
231	
232	## Verifying the Output
233	
234	Standard git tooling works against the converted repo without
235	additional flags — the `extensions.objectformat` setting in the local
236	config is enough for git to switch hashing:
237	
238	```bash
239	git -C /path/to/out.git fsck --full                     # zero errors expected
240	git -C /path/to/out.git config extensions.objectformat  # prints sha256
241	git -C /path/to/out.git log --oneline -5                # SHA256 hashes
242	git -C /path/to/out.git log --notes=sha1-origin -5      # with original SHA1
243	```
244	
245	To use the result as a working repo:
246	
247	```bash
248	git clone /path/to/out.git /path/to/checkout
249	```
250	
251	To serve it from a host that accepts SHA256:
252	
253	```bash
254	git -C /path/to/out.git push --mirror <new-remote-url>
255	```
256	
257	## Implementation Notes
258	
259	The translator works in three phases:
260	
261	1. Pack fetch into a filesystem-backed SHA1 storer, via go-git's pack
262	   parser, so deltas are resolved up front and the SHA1 source is
263	   randomly addressable thereafter.
264	2. Memoized recursive DFS from each desired ref tip. Blobs are copied
265	   as-is and re-hashed; trees, commits, and tags are decoded, their
266	   embedded hashes rewritten via the SHA1 → SHA256 mapping, signatures
267	   stripped, messages optionally rewritten, then re-encoded. Each
268	   translated object is written as a loose object under
269	   `objects/<aa>/<rest>` in the target.
270	3. Refs and HEAD are written at the translated tip hashes; the origin
271	   notes commit (if enabled) is built and stored under
272	   `refs/notes/sha1-origin`; the mapping file (if requested) is
273	   written.
274	
275	Note: loose object writing is done by hand rather than via go-git's
276	`SetEncodedObject`. The underlying `plumbing/format/objfile.Writer`
277	in `go-git/v6@v6.0.0-alpha.3` hardcodes SHA1 in its hasher, which
278	would put every translated object at a SHA1-derived path even though
279	the content references SHA256. This is verified by a unit test that
280	recomputes `sha256` of every loose object's decompressed content and
281	compares against the filename.
282	
283	## When Not to Use This
284	
285	- If the source repo will continue to receive commits and you need
286	  to keep mirroring them, use `git-sync sync` instead. This command
287	  is one-off; running it twice gives you two unrelated SHA256 repos.
288	- If you need to preserve GPG-verifiable history, conversion is not
289	  the right tool — every signature is invalidated by definition.
290	- If your downstream consumers cannot handle SHA256 (e.g. tools
291	  reading commit hashes from your hosting forge's API), wait until
292	  that side adds support.
293

Anything outside the repo — PR descriptions, issue trackers, deploy

input.new_string:
## Implementation Notes

The translator works in four phases:

1. **Pack fetch.** A single self-contained pack is streamed into a
   filesystem-backed SHA1 storer via go-git's pack parser, so deltas
   are resolved up front and the SHA1 source is randomly addressable
   for the rest of the run.

2. **Discovery.** A non-encoding DFS walks every object reachable
   from each desired ref tip via tree entries, commit
   tree+parent links, and tag targets. Each visited SHA1 is recorded
   in a `reachable map[Hash]ObjectType`. This set is the authoritative
   "what is in scope" answer used by both submodule resolution and
   message-reference rewriting — uniqueness of abbreviated SHA1
   prefixes is decided against this set once, never against the
   in-flight mapping.

3. **Translation.** Memoized recursive DFS from each desired ref tip.
   Blobs are copied as-is and re-hashed; trees, commits, and tags are
   decoded, their embedded hashes rewritten via the SHA1 → SHA256
   mapping, signatures stripped, and messages rewritten. The DFS
   recursion includes message-reference edges: for each commit or
   tag whose message mentions a SHA1 of a commit or tag in the
   reachable set, that referenced object is translated first. This
   guarantees the mapping is populated before the substitution
   happens, so cross-branch references resolve as reliably as
   ancestor references. Each translated object is written as a loose
   object under `objects/<aa>/<rest>` in the target.

4. **Refs and side outputs.** Refs and HEAD are written at the
   translated tip hashes; the origin notes commit (if enabled) is
   built and stored under `refs/notes/sha1-origin`; the mapping file
   (if requested) is written.

A defensive `inProgress` set guards against cycles during phase 3.
Real Git histories cannot form cycles (parent, tree, and tag-target
edges are a DAG by construction, and SHA1 message-reference cycles
are cryptographically infeasible), so a trip into this branch is a
hard error rather than a silent skip.

Note: loose object writing is done by hand rather than via go-git's
`SetEncodedObject`. The underlying `plumbing/format/objfile.Writer`
in `go-git/v6@v6.0.0-alpha.3` hardcodes SHA1 in its hasher, which
would put every translated object at a SHA1-derived path even though
the content references SHA256. This is verified by a unit test that
recomputes `sha256` of every loose object's decompressed content and
compares against the filename.

content.text:
All done.

**Code (`cmd/git-sync/internal/sha256convert/sha256convert.go`):**
- New `discover` phase walks every reachable object from desired ref tips once and populates a `reachable map[Hash]ObjectType` before any encoding starts.
- `resolveMessageRef` decides uniqueness against `reachable` (filtered to commits/tags), so abbreviated-prefix verdicts are fixed once and never depend on iteration order.
- `extractMessageReferences` returns the in-scope commit/tag SHA1s mentioned by a message.
- `translateCommit` and `translateTag` recurse via `translate()` on every in-scope message reference *before* rewriting the message, making message refs part of the translation DFS's topological order. By the time substitution runs, every reference is in the mapping.
- `translate()` keeps an `inProgress` set as a defensive cycle guard. (Cycles in this graph are cryptographically infeasible, so this should never fire — but if it does, we get an error instead of a stack overflow.)
- `Run()` calls `tr.discover(...)` before the translate loop.

**Tests:**
- All existing tests updated to call `tr.discover(...)` before `tr.translate(...)`.
- New `TestTranslator_RewritesCrossBranchReferences` constructs two sibling branches with no common ancestry where commit B references commit A by full and abbreviated SHA1. Translating B *first* (the order under which the old design would have stranded the rewrite) still produces a B whose message contains A's SHA256.
- Integration test against `git-http-backend` still passes.

**Docs (`docs/convert-sha256.md`):**
- "What It Does" now lists the four phases (fetch, discover, translate, refs/sides).
- The "Limitation: cross-branch references…" paragraph is gone, replaced with a "Two design notes" block that documents the topological-edge mechanism and the cryptographic-impossibility argument for why cycles aren't a concern.
- "External SHA1 references break silently" is reworded — references inside the repo now resolve regardless of branch.
- "Implementation Notes" expanded to describe the discovery pass and the message-reference edge mechanism.

**Real-world smoke (cobra):** identical numbers to before (1117 commits, 13 rewrites) since cobra's existing references happen to be ancestor-only. The unit test is what proves the new design actually fixes cross-branch refs; this would also catch any regression.
deleted a few paragraphs from the docs
Acknowledged.
Tell me more about the remaining limitations

Here's what's still imperfect, grouped by how fundamental they are:

**Things you can't fix in this tool (they're properties of the rewrite):**

- **GPG signatures are gone.** Every signed commit becomes unsigned. Can't be re-signed automatically without misattributing authorship; if you want signed history, see the conversation about re-signing earlier.
- **`mergetag` headers are dropped.** A merge commit's `mergetag` extra header embeds the signed-tag content of the tag being merged. Dropping it strips that nested signature but also loses the tag content from the merge commit's bytes. Visible via `git cat-file -p <merge>`: the tag block is just missing.
- **No hosting forge accepts SHA256 pushes today.** GitHub, GitLab.com, Bitbucket all advertise SHA1 only. Self-hosted Gitea/Forgejo + plain `git http-backend` work.
- **External SHA1 references** (PR descriptions, Jira tickets, container labels, deploy logs, dashboard links) — the converter has no way to reach them. The mapping file + your own scripts is the only path.

**Things that survive as data but no longer behave correctly:**

- **`refs/notes/*` copied under `--all-refs`** — the *tree paths inside* a notes tree are SHA1-hex strings encoding the commit each note attaches to. We translate the notes tree as a tree (entries get SHA256 blob refs), but we don't rename the entries from SHA1 to SHA256, so `git notes show <sha256>` won't find them. Notes are inert.
- **`refs/replace/<sha1>` refs** — the SHA1 is in the ref name itself, which we copy verbatim. Git looks for `refs/replace/<sha256>` and never finds it, so replacements stop applying.
- **Reflog isn't preserved.** Brand-new bare repo, no operational history.

**Things that depend on what you ran:**

- **In-message references only resolve within scope.** If `--branch main` excludes a feature branch, and a commit on main says "cherry-picked from abc1234" where abc1234 is on the feature branch, that reference isn't rewritten — abc1234 isn't in the reachable set. Use `--all-refs` for maximum coverage.
- **HEAD can dangle.** If your scope excludes the branch source HEAD points to, the target's HEAD stays at the PlainInit default (`refs/heads/master`) and resolves to nothing. Either include that branch or fix HEAD manually with `git symbolic-ref HEAD refs/heads/<branch>`.
- **Submodule gitlinks pointing outside the repo abort the run.** A SHA1 cannot be embedded in a SHA256 tree. Exclude affected refs or convert the submodule repository first.
- **Ambiguous abbreviated SHA1s in messages are left alone.** If `abc12` matches two commits in scope, we don't rewrite. Look these up via the mapping file. (Rare on hex prefixes ≥ 7 chars within a single repo.)

**Things to keep in mind operationally:**

- **No incremental updates.** The command is one-off. If the source keeps receiving commits, you re-convert from scratch — and the notes commit will have a different SHA256 across runs (its `committer` timestamp is `time.Now()`), so even the *exact same source state* produces different `refs/notes/sha1-origin` hashes. All user-visible refs (`refs/heads/*`, `refs/tags/*`) *are* deterministic.
- **Storage is all loose objects.** One file per object. Run `git gc --aggressive` afterwards or filesystems with poor small-file performance will hurt.
- **Memory scales with reachable object count.** Roughly two `map[Hash]…` structures of size N: the `reachable` set built by discovery and the `mapping` built during translation. ~2 GB peak on the Linux kernel, KB on cobra-sized repos. The discovery pass adds an extra full decode of every reachable object (no encoding), so total work is roughly 1.5× a non-rewriting conversion.
- **Abbreviated-prefix uniqueness check is a linear scan over `reachable` per match.** Fine up to ~100k commits, slower past that. Mentioned as a TODO in the code if it ever bites.

**The one place this leaves you exposed:**

If you're using a forge that auto-links short hashes (GitHub does this in issues, PRs, releases), then even after conversion the references *inside* your repo's messages will resolve (they're SHA256 now), but anything *outside* your repo still points at SHA1. There's no path to a clean cutover without coordinated rewrites on the consumer side. The realistic posture for most teams is: keep the SHA1 repo around read-only as a forwarding address for old references, and start fresh on the SHA256 side.
I think it would make sense to always convert all branches and tags

Agreed — and it cleans up a real footgun: with `--branch main`, a cherry-pick reference on main pointing into the excluded feature branch silently doesn't resolve. The "in-scope filtering" limitation we documented is self-inflicted by the flag.

Concretely, if we go this route:

**Remove:**
- `--branch` — always all branches.
- `--tags` — tags always included.
- `--map` — ref renaming doesn't fit a one-off cutover.

**Keep, with one decision to make:**
- `--exclude-ref-prefix` is still useful, but mostly for excluding `refs/pull/*` on GitHub mirrors. Worth keeping.
- `--all-refs` — currently opt-in to notes/pulls/replace/custom namespaces. Three options:

  1. **Drop it, always default to branches + tags only.** Simple, but `refs/notes/*`, Gerrit's `refs/changes/*`, `refs/replace/*`, etc. require running `git-sync` on the source to get them (or a manual fetch). Probably fine — those refs are mostly broken under SHA256 anyway (notes/replace encode SHA1s in their content/names).
  2. **Drop it, always do every `refs/*` on the source.** Maximizes fidelity but pulls in tens of thousands of `refs/pull/*` on GitHub by default. Annoying.
  3. **Keep it as the only scope knob.** Default branches+tags, `--all-refs` to opt into everything, `--exclude-ref-prefix` to subtract.

  I'd lean (3) — same shape as now, just with the source-filtering flags removed.

**Other observations from this change:**
- The "in-message refs only resolve within scope" limitation in the docs disappears, since branches and tags are always all in scope.
- The `--help` text gets shorter and harder to misuse.
- The integration test gets simpler (no scope flags to set up).

Sound right? If yes, which of the three options for `--all-refs`?
Sounds ok, option 3 for all-refa

90		cmd.Flags().BoolVar(&req.IncludeTags, "tags", false, "include annotated and lightweight tags")

99		cmd.Flags().BoolVar(&req.SkipMessageRewrite, "no-rewrite-messages", false,

package main

import (
	"errors"
	"fmt"

	gitsync "entire.io/entire/git-sync"
	"entire.io/entire/git-sync/cmd/git-sync/internal/sha256convert"
	"github.com/spf13/cobra"
)

func newConvertSHA256Cmd() *cobra.Command {
	var (
		req         = sha256convert.Request{}
		jsonOutput  bool
		protocolVal = newProtocolFlag()
	)

	cmd := &cobra.Command{
		Use:   "convert-sha256 [flags] <source-url> <target-dir>",
		Short: "One-off SHA1 → SHA256 conversion of a remote repo into a local bare repo",
		Long: `convert-sha256 fetches a pack from a SHA1 HTTP source and writes a new
SHA256 bare repository on disk at <target-dir>. Every reachable object is
re-hashed under SHA256 and tree/commit/tag references are rewritten.

All branches and tags on the source are always converted — partial scope
risks stranding cross-branch references in commit messages. Pass
--all-refs to also include refs/notes/*, refs/pull/*, and other custom
namespaces; pass --exclude-ref-prefix to subtract specific namespaces
from --all-refs.

			if req.TargetDir == "" && len(args) > 1 {
				req.TargetDir = args[1]
			}
			if req.SourceURL == "" || req.TargetDir == "" {
				return errors.New("convert-sha256 requires a source URL and a target directory")
			}

			if err != nil {
				return fmt.Errorf("convert-sha256: %w", err)
			}
			printOutput(jsonOutput, result)
			return nil
		},
	}

	cmd.Flags().StringVar(&req.SourceURL, "source-url", "", "source repository URL")
	cmd.Flags().BoolVar(&req.SourceFollowInfoRefsRedirect, "source-follow-info-refs-redirect",

		"send follow-up source RPCs to the final /info/refs redirect host")
	cmd.Flags().StringVar(&req.SourceAuth.Token, "source-token",

	cmd.Flags().StringVar(&req.SourceAuth.Username, "source-username",
		envOr("GITSYNC_SOURCE_USERNAME", "git"), "source basic auth username")
	cmd.Flags().StringVar(&req.SourceAuth.BearerToken, "source-bearer-token",
		envOr("GITSYNC_SOURCE_BEARER_TOKEN", ""), "source bearer token")
	cmd.Flags().BoolVar(&req.SourceAuth.SkipTLSVerify, "source-insecure-skip-tls-verify",
		envBool("GITSYNC_SOURCE_INSECURE_SKIP_TLS_VERIFY"),
		"skip TLS certificate verification for the source")
	cmd.Flags().StringVar(&req.TargetDir, "target-dir", "", "directory to initialize as a SHA256 bare repository")

	allRefsFlag(cmd, allRefsUsageScopeOnly, &req.AllRefs)
	excludeRefPrefixFlag(cmd, &req.ExcludeRefPrefixes)
	addProtocolFlag(cmd, &protocolVal)
	cmd.Flags().BoolVarP(&req.Verbose, "verbose", "v", false, "verbose logging")
	cmd.Flags().BoolVar(&req.KeepSourceObjects, "keep-source-objects", false,
		"keep the temporary SHA1 store on disk after conversion (for debugging)")
	cmd.Flags().StringVar(&req.MappingFile, "write-mapping", "",
		"write the full SHA1 → SHA256 mapping as a TSV to this path; useful for rewriting external references")
	cmd.Flags().BoolVar(&req.SkipMessageRewrite, "no-rewrite-messages", false,
		"do not rewrite SHA1 hash references found in commit and tag messages")
	cmd.Flags().BoolVar(&req.SkipOriginNotes, "no-origin-notes", false,
		"do not write a refs/notes/sha1-origin ref recording each commit's original SHA1")
	cmd.Flags().BoolVar(&jsonOutput, "json", false, "print JSON output")

input.old_string:
## Quick Start

```bash
git-sync convert-sha256 --tags \
  https://github.com/source-org/source-repo.git \
  /path/to/out.git
```

The target directory must not exist or must be empty. The result is a bare
repository with `extensions.objectformat = sha256` and a `refs/notes/sha1-origin`
ref recording each commit's pre-conversion SHA1.

For a private source, pass the token via the environment so it isn't
exposed in `ps`:

```bash
GITSYNC_SOURCE_TOKEN=ghp_xxx git-sync convert-sha256 --tags \
  https://github.com/source-org/private-repo.git \
  /path/to/out.git
```
input.new_string:
## Quick Start

```bash
git-sync convert-sha256 \
  https://github.com/source-org/source-repo.git \
  /path/to/out.git
```

Every branch and every tag on the source is always converted — there is no
flag to scope by branch, because a partial conversion risks stranding
cross-branch references in commit messages. The target directory must not
exist or must be empty. The result is a bare repository with
`extensions.objectformat = sha256` and a `refs/notes/sha1-origin` ref
recording each commit's pre-conversion SHA1.

For a private source, pass the token via the environment so it isn't
exposed in `ps`:

```bash
GITSYNC_SOURCE_TOKEN=ghp_xxx git-sync convert-sha256 \
  https://github.com/source-org/private-repo.git \
  /path/to/out.git
```

Pass `--all-refs` to also include `refs/notes/*`, `refs/pull/*`, and
other custom namespaces; pair with `--exclude-ref-prefix` to subtract
specific namespaces (e.g. `--exclude-ref-prefix refs/pull/` on GitHub
mirrors).

198	
199	**Submodule gitlinks must resolve in-repo.** Tree entries with mode
200	`160000` reference a commit in another repository, but a SHA1 hash
201	cannot be embedded in a SHA256 tree. The command translates the
202	pointer if the referenced commit happens to live in the same store
203	(rare; sometimes seen in vendored modules), and otherwise exits with
204	an error naming the offending tree, entry, and hash. Scope around
205	those refs with `--exclude-ref-prefix` or `--branch`, or convert the
206	submodule repository first.
207	
208	**External SHA1 references break silently.** See the section above for
209	mitigations. References inside the repo (commit and tag messages) are
210	rewritten when they uniquely identify a commit or tag in scope.
211	Anything outside the repo — PR descriptions, issue trackers, deploy
212	manifests, container labels — is not the converter's job; use the
213	mapping file to drive those rewrites.
214	
215	**Replace refs and notes refs become detached.** `refs/replace/<sha1>`
216	encodes a SHA1 in the ref name itself, so the name doesn't match
217	under SHA256 and the replacement never triggers. `refs/notes/*` paths
218	encode the target object's hash as a tree path, so existing notes
219	copied under `--all-refs` survive as data but no longer attach to
220	their original commits. Neither is a correctness issue, just lost
221	behavior.
222	
223	**HEAD can dangle.** When the source's symbolic HEAD branch is not in
224	the desired ref set, the target's HEAD is left at `refs/heads/master`
225	(go-git's PlainInit default) and resolves to nothing. Either include
226	the HEAD branch in scope or set it manually after conversion with
227	`git -C <target> symbolic-ref HEAD refs/heads/<branch>`.
228	
229	**Storage is all loose objects.** The command writes one file per
230	object. Correct, but on filesystems that dislike millions of small
231	files this is slow. Run `git -C <target> gc --aggressive` afterwards
232	to pack the converted repo down to a single packfile.

convert-sha256 fetches a pack from a SHA1 HTTP source and writes a new
SHA256 bare repository on disk at <target-dir>. Every reachable object is
re-hashed under SHA256 and tree/commit/tag references are rewritten.

All branches and tags on the source are always converted — partial scope
risks stranding cross-branch references in commit messages. Pass
--all-refs to also include refs/notes/*, refs/pull/*, and other custom
namespaces; pass --exclude-ref-prefix to subtract specific namespaces
from --all-refs.

The conversion is destructive in two ways the caller should be aware of:
GPG signatures on commits and tags are dropped (they sign over the
original SHA1 content and would be invalid post-rewrite), and submodule
gitlinks that point at a commit outside this repository cannot be
embedded in a SHA256 tree — the command exits with an error if it finds
any so the caller can convert the submodule repository first.

Usage:
  git-sync convert-sha256 [flags] <source-url> <target-dir>

Flags:
      --all-refs                           include every refs/* on the source (notes, pulls, custom namespaces) — scope only, no failure-handling effect
      --exclude-ref-prefix stringArray     exclude refs whose names start with this prefix; repeatable. Subtracts from auto-discovery (branches/tags/--all-refs); explicit --map values are not subject to this filter
  -h, --help                               help for convert-sha256
      --json                               print JSON output
      --keep-source-objects                keep the temporary SHA1 store on disk after conversion (for debugging)
      --no-origin-notes                    do not write a refs/notes/sha1-origin ref recording each commit's original SHA1
      --no-rewrite-messages                do not rewrite SHA1 hash references found in commit and tag messages
      --protocol string                    protocol mode: auto, v1, or v2 (default "auto")
      --source-bearer-token string         source bearer token
      --source-follow-info-refs-redirect   send follow-up source RPCs to the final /info/refs redirect host
      --source-insecure-skip-tls-verify    skip TLS certificate verification for the source
      --source-token string                source token/password
      --source-url string                  source repository URL
      --source-username string             source basic auth username (default "git")

content.text:
Done. Summary:

**CLI (`cmd/git-sync/convert_sha256.go`):**
- Removed `--branch`, `--tags`, `--map`.
- `--all-refs` and `--exclude-ref-prefix` retained per option 3.
- Help text now spells out the fixed-scope behavior and the role of `--all-refs`.

**Library (`cmd/git-sync/internal/sha256convert/sha256convert.go`):**
- `Request` no longer has `Branches`, `IncludeTags`, or `Mappings` fields.
- `Run()` always sets `IncludeTags: true` in the planner config.
- `toPlannerMappings` helper deleted.

**Docs (`docs/convert-sha256.md`):**
- Quick Start no longer uses `--tags`; gains a paragraph explaining that scope is fixed and pointing at `--all-refs` / `--exclude-ref-prefix`.
- "What It Does" step 1 rewritten to match the new scope rules.
- Flags reference block trimmed and now ends with an explicit note that `--branch`, `--tags`, and `--map` don't exist.
- Submodule paragraph no longer suggests `--branch` as a workaround.
- "HEAD can dangle" paragraph rewritten — under the fixed scope it no longer can; the old behavior is mentioned only as historical context.

**Verified:**
- All unit and integration tests pass (`go test ./...` clean).
- A real run against cobra without any scope flags now picks up all 37 refs automatically (was 8 with the previous default of "branches only").
- `git fsck --full` clean on the result.
Can you summarize again the limitations?

Now that scope is fixed (every branch + every tag, always), several limitations went away. Here's the updated set:

**Lost permanently (no automated fix):**
- **GPG signatures are stripped.** Every signed commit becomes unsigned. Same for signed annotated tags. Re-signing all history with one key is possible but misattributes authorship; we explicitly don't do it.
- **`mergetag` headers are dropped** from merge commits, removing the nested signed-tag block from the merge's bytes.

**Survive as data but stop behaving correctly:**
- **`refs/notes/*` copied via `--all-refs`** — tree paths inside notes trees are SHA1-hex strings encoding the commit each note attaches to. The notes survive but `git notes show <sha256>` won't find them. Use the tool's own `refs/notes/sha1-origin` instead.
- **`refs/replace/<sha1>` refs** — the SHA1 is in the ref name itself; replacements no longer trigger under SHA256.
- **Reflog isn't preserved.** New bare repo, no operational history.

**Edge cases inside this repo:**
- **Submodule gitlinks pointing outside the repo abort the run.** A SHA1 can't be embedded in a SHA256 tree. Convert the submodule repository first, then retry.
- **Ambiguous abbreviated SHA1 references in messages are left unrewritten.** If `abc12` matches two commits in scope (rare on prefixes ≥ 7 chars), we don't substitute — look them up via the mapping file.

**Outside the repo (the big one):**
- **External SHA1 references break silently.** PR descriptions, issue trackers, dashboards, container labels, deploy manifests. The converter has no reach into those systems; the `--write-mapping` TSV is the only way to drive bulk rewrites elsewhere.
- **No hosting forge accepts SHA256 pushes today.** GitHub, GitLab.com, Bitbucket all advertise SHA1 only. Self-hosted Gitea/Forgejo and plain `git http-backend` work.

**Operational characteristics worth knowing:**
- **One-off, not incremental.** Re-running creates a fresh repo; if the source kept committing, you re-convert from scratch. Object hashes for branches/tags are deterministic across runs, but the `refs/notes/sha1-origin` commit isn't — its `committer` timestamp is `time.Now()`, so that ref's hash will differ between runs.
- **Storage is all loose objects.** Millions of tiny files on slow filesystems hurt. Run `git -C <target> gc --aggressive` afterwards.
- **Memory scales linearly with reachable object count.** Two `map[Hash]…` structures (reachable + mapping). ~2 GB peak on a Linux-kernel-scale repo, KB on cobra-scale.
- **Discovery adds a non-encoding decode pass.** Total work ≈ 1.5× a non-rewriting conversion.
- **Abbreviated-prefix lookup is a linear scan** over reachable per match. Fine to ~100k commits, slower past that.

**Gone since the last summary:**
- ~~HEAD can dangle~~ — every branch is always converted, so the source's symbolic HEAD branch is always present and HEAD is always repointed correctly.
- ~~In-message refs only resolve within scope~~ — all branches and tags are always in scope, and message-reference edges in the translation DFS make cross-branch references resolve as reliably as ancestor ones.

`160000` reference a commit in another repository, but a SHA1 hash

**Submodule gitlinks must resolve in-repo (fail-fast).** Tree entries
with mode `160000` reference a commit in another repository, but a
SHA1 hash cannot be embedded in a SHA256 tree. The discovery pass
checks every tree before any object is written, so an unresolvable
submodule errors out *before* the target bare repo is initialized —
the target directory is left as the caller found it. The error names
the offending tree, entry, and hash. Convert the submodule repository
first so its commit hashes are available in a SHA256-compatible form
before retrying.

All green. Summary of changes:

**Submodule fail-fast (`sha256convert.go`):**
- Extracted `discoverReachable` as a standalone function (returns `map[Hash]ObjectType`, no longer a translator method).
- When discovery encounters a tree entry with `filemode.Submodule` whose hash isn't in the source store, it returns an error immediately naming the offending tree, entry, and hash.
- Reordered `Run()` so the target bare repo is initialized *after* discovery succeeds. A failed run now leaves the target directory unchanged from the user's perspective (it was only ensured empty).

**Ambiguous-prefix warning (`sha256convert.go`):**
- Replaced the boolean return of `resolveMessageRef` with a `matchResult` tri-state (`matchNone` / `matchUnique` / `matchAmbiguous`).
- Added `translator.ambiguousMessageRefs map[string]struct{}`. `rewriteHashesInMessage` records every prefix that hit `matchAmbiguous`.
- Added `AmbiguousMessageRefs []string` to `Result`, sorted on output.
- `Lines()` now emits a warning listing up to 5 ambiguous prefixes with a `(N more)` tail. `--json` exposes the full list under `ambiguousMessageRefs`.

**Tests (`sha256convert_test.go`):**
- All callers updated to the new shape: `discoverReachable(src, roots)` then `newTranslator(src, dst, dir, rewriteMessages, reachable)`.
- `TestTranslator_UnresolvableSubmodule` rewritten to assert the error surfaces in `discoverReachable` (not in translation).
- New `TestTranslator_AmbiguousMessageRefWarning` injects two synthetic reachable SHA1s sharing the prefix `deadbee` and verifies the prefix is left in the message and recorded in `ambiguousMessageRefs`.

**Docs (`docs/convert-sha256.md`):**
- Submodule sharp-edge paragraph now says "fail-fast" and notes the target dir is left untouched.
- New paragraph in the inline-rewriting section documents the ambiguous-prefix behavior and the warning surface.

**Lost permanently (no automated fix):**
- **GPG signatures are stripped.** Every signed commit becomes unsigned. Same for signed annotated tags. Re-signing all history with one key is possible but misattributes authorship; we explicitly don't do it.
- **`mergetag` headers are dropped** from merge commits, removing the nested signed-tag block from the merge's bytes.

**Edge cases inside this repo:**
- **Submodule gitlinks pointing outside the repo abort the run, fail-fast.** Caught during discovery, before the target bare repo is initialized. The target directory is left untouched. Convert the submodule repository first, then retry.
- **Ambiguous abbreviated SHA1 references in messages are left unrewritten, with a warning.** If `abc12` matches two commits in scope (rare on prefixes ≥ 7 chars), we don't substitute and the run prints the offending prefixes (full list in `--json` output). Look them up via the mapping file.

**Operational characteristics worth knowing:**
- **One-off, not incremental.** Re-running creates a fresh repo; if the source kept committing, you re-convert from scratch. - **Discovery adds a non-encoding decode pass.** Total work ≈ 1.5× a non-rewriting conversion.

**Lost permanently:**

**Operational:**

ok let's look at mergetag, what's that about?

When you `git merge` a signed annotated tag (e.g. `git merge v1.2.3`), git embeds the entire signed tag object — header, body, *and PGP signature* — as a `mergetag` extra header on the resulting merge commit. The point is to preserve the signature attestation: a future reader of the merge commit can still verify the tag was signed by the original author, even though the merge commit itself is unsigned.

A typical merge commit object looks like:

```
tree <hash>
parent <hash1>
parent <hash2>
author Linus Torvalds ...
committer Linus Torvalds ...
mergetag object 4a2c4f6b1e9d... 
 type commit
 tag v1.2.3
 tagger Greg KH ... 1700000000 -0700

 Linux 1.2.3
 -----BEGIN PGP SIGNATURE-----
 iQEzBAABCAAdFiEE...
 -----END PGP SIGNATURE-----

Merge tag 'v1.2.3' into master
```

The space-prefixed lines after `mergetag object` are the whole signed tag's bytes embedded verbatim. This is the standard Linux kernel pattern: maintainers send signed pull-request tags, Linus merges them, and the mergetag header makes each merge commit self-attesting.

**Why we drop it.** That embedded block contains two things our rewrite breaks:

1. The `object <sha1>` line names a commit by its **SHA1** hash. After conversion that hash doesn't exist; the reference is dangling.
2. The PGP signature was computed over the original tag's bytes — including those SHA1 references. We can't rewrite the SHA1s to SHA256 *and* keep the signature valid; any rewrite invalidates the signature, same as with regular commit signatures.

So we have the same three options as with direct commit signatures:

- **Drop the header.** Clean merge commit, lost attestation. (What we do.)
- **Keep it with rewritten hashes.** Signature now invalid; `git verify-commit` complains. Misleading.
- **Keep it with original SHA1 hashes.** Internal `object <sha1>` reference is a dangling SHA1 inside an otherwise-SHA256 repo. Even more misleading.

**What's actually lost.** The signed tag itself still exists in the converted repo — just stripped of its own signature, same as every other signed object. The merge commit's *human-readable* message ("Merge tag 'v1.2.3' into master") survives in the commit body. The only loss is the cryptographic attestation that the merge incorporates a signed tag. Functionally equivalent to losing every other GPG signature; it's just an extra place they hide.

If your workflow leans heavily on mergetag verification (kernel-style "pull from a signed maintainer tag"), SHA256 conversion is genuinely lossy for that audit chain — there's no fix short of re-signing the whole history with a new key, which has its own correctness problems.
ok let's leave that for now, let's goo through the operational section can you give me a summary of each item

**1. One-off, not incremental.**
`convert-sha256` produces a new SHA256 repo from scratch every time. There's no "fetch the new SHA1 commits from upstream and convert just those, appending to the existing SHA256 repo." If your source repo keeps receiving commits after you convert, you either run it again from zero (losing reference stability — see #2) or you accept that the converted repo is a frozen snapshot. Realistic use: convert once, then make the converted repo the new canonical store and accept new commits directly into it.

**2. `refs/notes/sha1-origin` commit hash non-deterministic across runs.**
Branch and tag hashes are deterministic — given the same source state, two runs produce identical SHA256s for every commit, tree, blob, and tag. But the notes commit we build has a `committer` line containing `time.Now()`, so its bytes differ between runs, so *its* SHA256 differs. The notes themselves (each commit's original SHA1 hex) are still the same data; only the wrapper commit's hash drifts. Matters if you're comparing two conversion outputs byte-for-byte; doesn't matter for anything you'd actually do with the converted repo.

**3. Loose-object storage; run `git gc --aggressive` afterwards.**
We write one file per object under `objects/<aa>/<rest>` — no pack file is produced. For cobra (~4500 objects), 4500 files. For the Linux kernel, millions of files. On SSDs nobody notices; on slow network filesystems or anything with bad small-file performance, it's painful. Fix is mechanical: `git gc --aggressive` packs everything into one packfile. We don't run it ourselves because it'd add minutes of post-processing to the run; you can decide whether you need it.

**4. Memory linear in reachable object count.**
Two big `map[plumbing.Hash]…` structures stay live for the whole run: `reachable` (SHA1 → object type, built by discovery) and `mapping` (SHA1 → SHA256, built by translation). Each entry is ~80 bytes accounting for the Go map overhead. At 16M objects (Linux kernel) that's ~2.5 GB peak; at cobra's ~5k objects, kilobytes. Linear and predictable. Could be cut roughly in half by interning SHA1s as 32-bit IDs, but at the cobra/typical-repo scale it's not worth the complexity.

**5. Discovery adds a ~1.5× decode pass.**
We decode every reachable object twice during a run: once in discovery (to build the reachable set + check submodule resolvability) and once in translation (to actually rewrite and encode). Discovery is decode-only, no encoding, so it's cheaper than translation — roughly half the cost. Net runtime is about 1.5× a hypothetical translation-only design. The win is that discovery is what makes the cross-branch rewrite work *and* what makes submodule errors fail-fast before any output is written. Worth paying.

**6. Abbreviated-prefix lookup is a linear scan over reachable per match.**
When a commit message contains an abbreviated SHA1 like `abc1234`, we check uniqueness by iterating every entry in `reachable` and counting prefix matches. Each abbreviated reference costs one scan. For cobra's 5k entries × 13 references = 65k iterations, microseconds. For a Linux-kernel scale repo with 16M entries × thousands of references = potentially minutes spent just in the prefix scan. There's a TODO in the code: build a sorted-by-hex-prefix index once and binary-search instead. Easy to add when someone hits it.
ok all of these are acceptable for now, are they documented too?
Let me check what's actually in the doc right now.

230	
231	**HEAD always resolves under the fixed scope.** Because every branch is
232	converted, the source's symbolic HEAD branch is always present in the

235

237	object. Correct, but on filesystems that dislike millions of small
238	files this is slow. Run `git -C <target> gc --aggressive` afterwards
239	to pack the converted repo down to a single packfile.
240	
241	## Verifying the Output
242	
243	Standard git tooling works against the converted repo without
244	additional flags — the `extensions.objectformat` setting in the local
245	config is enough for git to switch hashing:
246	
247	```bash
248	git -C /path/to/out.git fsck --full                     # zero errors expected
249	git -C /path/to/out.git config extensions.objectformat  # prints sha256
250	git -C /path/to/out.git log --oneline -5                # SHA256 hashes
251	git -C /path/to/out.git log --notes=sha1-origin -5      # with original SHA1
252	```
253	
254	To use the result as a working repo:
255	
256	```bash
257	git clone /path/to/out.git /path/to/checkout
258	```
259	
260	To serve it from a host that accepts SHA256:
261	
262	```bash
263	git -C /path/to/out.git push --mirror <new-remote-url>
264	```
265	
266	## Implementation Notes
267	
268	The translator works in four phases:
269	
270	1. **Pack fetch.** A single self-contained pack is streamed into a
271	   filesystem-backed SHA1 storer via go-git's pack parser, so deltas
272	   are resolved up front and the SHA1 source is randomly addressable
273	   for the rest of the run.
274	
275	2. **Discovery.** A non-encoding DFS walks every object reachable
276	   from each desired ref tip via tree entries, commit
277	   tree+parent links, and tag targets. Each visited SHA1 is recorded
278	   in a `reachable map[Hash]ObjectType`. This set is the authoritative
279	   "what is in scope" answer used by both submodule resolution and
280	   message-reference rewriting — uniqueness of abbreviated SHA1
281	   prefixes is decided against this set once, never against the
282	   in-flight mapping.
283	
284	3. **Translation.** Memoized recursive DFS from each desired ref tip.
285	   Blobs are copied as-is and re-hashed; trees, commits, and tags are
286	   decoded, their embedded hashes rewritten via the SHA1 → SHA256
287	   mapping, signatures stripped, and messages rewritten. The DFS
288	   recursion includes message-reference edges: for each commit or
289	   tag whose message mentions a SHA1 of a commit or tag in the
290	   reachable set, that referenced object is translated first. This
291	   guarantees the mapping is populated before the substitution
292	   happens, so cross-branch references resolve as reliably as
293	   ancestor references. Each translated object is written as a loose
294	   object under `objects/<aa>/<rest>` in the target.
295	
296	4. **Refs and side outputs.** Refs and HEAD are written at the
297	   translated tip hashes; the origin notes commit (if enabled) is
298	   built and stored under `refs/notes/sha1-origin`; the mapping file
299	   (if requested) is written.
300	
301	A defensive `inProgress` set guards against cycles during phase 3.
302	Real Git histories cannot form cycles (parent, tree, and tag-target
303	edges are a DAG by construction, and SHA1 message-reference cycles
304	are cryptographically infeasible), so a trip into this branch is a
305	hard error rather than a silent skip.
306	
307	Note: loose object writing is done by hand rather than via go-git's
308	`SetEncodedObject`. The underlying `plumbing/format/objfile.Writer`
309	in `go-git/v6@v6.0.0-alpha.3` hardcodes SHA1 in its hasher, which
310	would put every translated object at a SHA1-derived path even though
311	the content references SHA256. This is verified by a unit test that
312	recomputes `sha256` of every loose object's decompressed content and
313	compares against the filename.

result.output:
1	# SHA1 → SHA256 Conversion
2	
3	`git-sync convert-sha256` is a one-off migration command that fetches a pack
4	from a SHA1 HTTP source and writes a new SHA256 bare repository on disk.
5	Every reachable object is re-hashed under SHA256 and tree, commit, and tag
6	references are rewritten accordingly.
7	
8	The command is intentionally narrow: it does not push to a remote, it does
9	not modify the source, and it is meant to be run once per repo. Resulting
10	SHA256 hashes have no relation to the original SHA1 hashes beyond a
11	mapping that the command can optionally emit.
12	
13	## Quick Start
14	
15	```bash
16	git-sync convert-sha256 \
17	  https://github.com/source-org/source-repo.git \
18	  /path/to/out.git
19	```
20	
21	Every branch and every tag on the source is always converted — there is no
22	flag to scope by branch, because a partial conversion risks stranding
23	cross-branch references in commit messages. The target directory must not
24	exist or must be empty. The result is a bare repository with
25	`extensions.objectformat = sha256` and a `refs/notes/sha1-origin` ref
26	recording each commit's pre-conversion SHA1.
27	
28	For a private source, pass the token via the environment so it isn't
29	exposed in `ps`:
30	
31	```bash
32	GITSYNC_SOURCE_TOKEN=ghp_xxx git-sync convert-sha256 \
33	  https://github.com/source-org/private-repo.git \
34	  /path/to/out.git
35	```
36	
37	Pass `--all-refs` to also include `refs/notes/*`, `refs/pull/*`, and
38	other custom namespaces; pair with `--exclude-ref-prefix` to subtract
39	specific namespaces (e.g. `--exclude-ref-prefix refs/pull/` on GitHub
40	mirrors).
41	
42	## What It Does
43	
44	1. Probes the source via smart HTTP and discovers every branch and tag,
45	   plus any additional namespaces opted in via `--all-refs` and not
46	   excluded by `--exclude-ref-prefix`.
47	2. Fetches a single self-contained pack via `upload-pack` and lands it in
48	   a temporary on-disk SHA1 bare repo. The temp directory is cleaned up
49	   on exit unless `--keep-source-objects` is passed.
50	3. Initializes the target as a bare SHA256 repository
51	   (`git init --object-format=sha256` equivalent).
52	4. Runs a **discovery pass** that walks every reachable object from
53	   each desired ref tip and records its SHA1 and object type. This
54	   gives the rewriter an authoritative "what's in scope" set so
55	   abbreviated message references can be resolved consistently and
56	   message-reference edges can be added to the translation graph.
57	5. Translates every reachable object in topological order via a
58	   memoized DFS:
59	   - **Blobs**: re-hashed under SHA256; content unchanged.
60	   - **Trees**: each entry's hash translated via the in-memory mapping;
61	     submodule gitlinks left as-is when the referenced commit is in
62	     this repo, otherwise the run errors.
63	   - **Commits**: `tree` and `parent` hashes translated; GPG signatures
64	     dropped; `mergetag` extra headers dropped; in-scope SHA1
65	     references in the message are translated first (so their SHA256s
66	     are known) and then substituted into the message.
67	   - **Tags**: target hash translated; signatures dropped; tag message
68	     hashes rewritten with the same edge mechanism as commits.
69	6. Writes refs in the SHA256 target at the translated tip hashes. HEAD
70	   is repointed at the source's symbolic HEAD when that ref made it
71	   into the conversion.
72	7. Optionally writes the SHA1 → SHA256 mapping as a TSV sidecar
73	   (`--write-mapping <path>`).
74	
75	The temp SHA1 store is on disk, not in memory, so peak RAM is bounded
76	by the in-memory mapping plus a small fixed delta-resolution cache.
77	Large repos still work; expect runtime dominated by the network fetch
78	and the loose-object write throughput.
79	
80	## Handling External SHA1 References
81	
82	A SHA1 → SHA256 cutover is destructive for external systems that
83	reference commits by hash: PR descriptions, issue trackers, deploy
84	logs, container labels, doc links, and so on all stop resolving. The
85	command offers three on-ramps for migrating those out of band.
86	
87	### 1. Inline message rewriting (default on)
88	
89	Commit and tag messages are scanned for 7-to-40-character hex runs.
90	When a run uniquely matches a commit or tag SHA1 in the conversion's
91	reachable set, it is replaced with the full SHA256 hex. Examples that
92	get rewritten:
93	
94	```
95	Reverts: a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2a3b4c5d6e7f8a9b0    →    full SHA256
96	Cherry-picked from a1b2c3d                            →    full SHA256
97	```
98	
99	Two design notes:
100	
101	- **Uniqueness is decided against the reachable set, not the
102	  in-flight mapping.** The discovery pass enumerates every reachable
103	  SHA1 before any encoding starts, so abbreviated prefixes get the
104	  same verdict regardless of how far the translation has progressed.
105	  If `a1b2c3d` matches two different commits in scope, it is treated
106	  as ambiguous and left alone — never rewritten on the basis of which
107	  one happened to be translated first.
108	- **Cross-branch references work the same as ancestor references.**
109	  Each in-scope SHA1 mentioned in a commit's message is added as a
110	  dependency edge in the translation DFS — that commit is translated
111	  before the referencing commit is encoded. So a cherry-pick from a
112	  sibling branch resolves just as reliably as a revert of an
113	  ancestor. (Cycles in this graph are cryptographically impossible:
114	  for both A's message to contain B's SHA1 and vice versa, you would
115	  need to know each hash before computing it.)
116	
117	False positives are essentially impossible because a run is only
118	substituted if its prefix uniquely matches a real SHA1 of a commit or
119	tag in scope. Blob and tree hashes are excluded from the match set
120	so incidental hex strings that collide with content hashes are not
121	rewritten. Disable with `--no-rewrite-messages` if you prefer
122	untouched messages.
123	
124	**Ambiguous prefixes are reported, not rewritten.** If `abc12` matches
125	two commits in scope, it is left as `abc12` in the output (rewriting
126	either would silently pick the wrong commit). The run prints a
127	warning line listing each ambiguous prefix and the `--json` output
128	includes them under `ambiguousMessageRefs`. Look them up in the
129	mapping file to decide which commit each one should point at.
130	
131	### 2. Origin notes ref (default on)
132	
133	`refs/notes/sha1-origin` is written after translation and holds, for
134	each translated commit, the pre-conversion SHA1 hex keyed by the new
135	SHA256 hash. Standard git tooling can read it:
136	
137	```bash
138	git -C /path/to/out.git notes --ref=sha1-origin show <sha256>
139	# prints the original SHA1
140	
141	git -C /path/to/out.git log --notes=sha1-origin
142	# shows the original SHA1 below each commit's body
143	```
144	
145	Notes attach meaningfully only to commits, so blobs, trees, and tags
146	are not represented in this ref. Disable with `--no-origin-notes`.
147	
148	### 3. Sidecar mapping file (opt in)
149	
150	`--write-mapping <path>` emits a TSV with one line per translated
151	object, sorted by SHA1:
152	
153	```
154	# sha1   sha256
155	00027b675386b21c4ca05316145671fb7034d251   d80415fa21bebb...
156	000bb155604d06f1c48fc7feb4b025d991ef3366   a23cf98db5abfa...
157	...
158	```
159	
160	Useful for bulk rewriting external systems: feed the file to a
161	script that walks Jira tickets, PR bodies, deploy manifests, or any
162	other system that holds frozen SHA1 references.
163	
164	## Flags
165	
166	```
167	--source-url                       source repository URL
168	--source-token                     source password/token (prefer env)
169	--source-username                  source basic auth username (default git)
170	--source-bearer-token              source bearer token
171	--source-insecure-skip-tls-verify  skip TLS verification (testing only)
172	--source-follow-info-refs-redirect follow /info/refs cross-host redirects
173	--target-dir                       SHA256 bare repo directory (must be empty)
174	
175	--all-refs                         also include refs/* outside heads/tags
176	                                   (notes, pulls, custom namespaces)
177	--exclude-ref-prefix               subtract refs by prefix; repeatable
178	
179	--protocol                         protocol mode (auto, v1, v2)
180	--write-mapping                    write SHA1 → SHA256 TSV to this path
181	--no-rewrite-messages              skip inline hash rewrites in messages
182	--no-origin-notes                  skip refs/notes/sha1-origin
183	--keep-source-objects              leave the temp SHA1 store on disk
184	--json                             machine-readable output
185	--verbose, -v                      verbose logging
186	```
187	
188	There are no `--branch`, `--tags`, or `--map` flags: scope is fixed to
189	every branch and every tag on the source.
190	
191	Environment fallbacks: `GITSYNC_SOURCE_TOKEN`, `GITSYNC_SOURCE_USERNAME`,
192	`GITSYNC_SOURCE_BEARER_TOKEN`, `GITSYNC_SOURCE_INSECURE_SKIP_TLS_VERIFY`,
193	`GITSYNC_SOURCE_FOLLOW_INFO_REFS_REDIRECT`, `GITSYNC_PROTOCOL`.
194	
195	## Sharp Edges
196	
197	**GPG signatures are stripped.** A signature is bytes signed over the
198	commit's pre-conversion content (including the SHA1 `tree` and
199	`parent` lines). After rewriting, the bytes no longer match the
200	signature, so verification would always fail. Rather than persist
201	invalid signatures, the command drops them and prints a warning count.
202	This matches upstream `git`'s own SHA256 conversion behavior. Tags
203	with embedded signatures and `mergetag` extra headers are handled the
204	same way.
205	
206	**Submodule gitlinks must resolve in-repo (fail-fast).** Tree entries
207	with mode `160000` reference a commit in another repository, but a
208	SHA1 hash cannot be embedded in a SHA256 tree. The discovery pass
209	checks every tree before any object is written, so an unresolvable
210	submodule errors out *before* the target bare repo is initialized —
211	the target directory is left as the caller found it. The error names
212	the offending tree, entry, and hash. Convert the submodule repository
213	first so its commit hashes are available in a SHA256-compatible form
214	before retrying.
215	
216	**External SHA1 references break silently.** See the section above for
217	mitigations. References inside the repo (commit and tag messages) are
218	rewritten when they uniquely identify a commit or tag in scope.
219	Anything outside the repo — PR descriptions, issue trackers, deploy
220	manifests, container labels — is not the converter's job; use the
221	mapping file to drive those rewrites.
222	
223	**Replace refs and notes refs become detached.** `refs/replace/<sha1>`
224	encodes a SHA1 in the ref name itself, so the name doesn't match
225	under SHA256 and the replacement never triggers. `refs/notes/*` paths
226	encode the target object's hash as a tree path, so existing notes
227	copied under `--all-refs` survive as data but no longer attach to
228	their original commits. Neither is a correctness issue, just lost
229	behavior.
230	
231	**HEAD always resolves under the fixed scope.** Because every branch is
232	converted, the source's symbolic HEAD branch is always present in the
233	target and HEAD is repointed at it. (Earlier versions of this tool
234	allowed `--branch` to scope, which could leave HEAD dangling.)
235	
236	**Storage is all loose objects.** The command writes one file per
237	object. Correct, but on filesystems that dislike millions of small
238	files this is slow. Run `git -C <target> gc --aggressive` afterwards
239	to pack the converted repo down to a single packfile.
240	
241	## Verifying the Output
242	
243	Standard git tooling works against the converted repo without
244	additional flags — the `extensions.objectformat` setting in the local
245	config is enough for git to switch hashing:
246	
247	```bash
248	git -C /path/to/out.git fsck --full                     # zero errors expected
249	git -C /path/to/out.git config extensions.objectformat  # prints sha256
250	git -C /path/to/out.git log --oneline -5                # SHA256 hashes
251	git -C /path/to/out.git log --notes=sha1-origin -5      # with original SHA1
252	```
253	
254	To use the result as a working repo:
255	
256	```bash
257	git clone /path/to/out.git /path/to/checkout
258	```
259	
260	To serve it from a host that accepts SHA256:
261	
262	```bash
263	git -C /path/to/out.git push --mirror <new-remote-url>
264	```
265	
266	## Implementation Notes
267	
268	The translator works in four phases:
269	
270	1. **Pack fetch.** A single self-contained pack is streamed into a
271	   filesystem-backed SHA1 storer via go-git's pack parser, so deltas
272	   are resolved up front and the SHA1 source is randomly addressable
273	   for the rest of the run.
274	
275	2. **Discovery.** A non-encoding DFS walks every object reachable
276	   from each desired ref tip via tree entries, commit
277	   tree+parent links, and tag targets. Each visited SHA1 is recorded
278	   in a `reachable map[Hash]ObjectType`. This set is the authoritative
279	   "what is in scope" answer used by both submodule resolution and
280	   message-reference rewriting — uniqueness of abbreviated SHA1
281	   prefixes is decided against this set once, never against the
282	   in-flight mapping.
283	
284	3. **Translation.** Memoized recursive DFS from each desired ref tip.
285	   Blobs are copied as-is and re-hashed; trees, commits, and tags are
286	   decoded, their embedded hashes rewritten via the SHA1 → SHA256
287	   mapping, signatures stripped, and messages rewritten. The DFS
288	   recursion includes message-reference edges: for each commit or
289	   tag whose message mentions a SHA1 of a commit or tag in the
290	   reachable set, that referenced object is translated first. This
291	   guarantees the mapping is populated before the substitution
292	   happens, so cross-branch references resolve as reliably as
293	   ancestor references. Each translated object is written as a loose
294	   object under `objects/<aa>/<rest>` in the target.
295	
296	4. **Refs and side outputs.** Refs and HEAD are written at the
297	   translated tip hashes; the origin notes commit (if enabled) is
298	   built and stored under `refs/notes/sha1-origin`; the mapping file
299	   (if requested) is written.
300	
301	A defensive `inProgress` set guards against cycles during phase 3.
302	Real Git histories cannot form cycles (parent, tree, and tag-target
303	edges are a DAG by construction, and SHA1 message-reference cycles
304	are cryptographically infeasible), so a trip into this branch is a
305	hard error rather than a silent skip.
306	
307	Note: loose object writing is done by hand rather than via go-git's
308	`SetEncodedObject`. The underlying `plumbing/format/objfile.Writer`
309	in `go-git/v6@v6.0.0-alpha.3` hardcodes SHA1 in its hasher, which
310	would put every translated object at a SHA1-derived path even though
311	the content references SHA256. This is verified by a unit test that
312	recomputes `sha256` of every loose object's decompressed content and
313	compares against the filename.
314
