input.content:
# SHA1 → SHA256 Conversion

`git-sync convert-sha256` is a one-off migration command that fetches a pack
from a SHA1 HTTP source and writes a new SHA256 bare repository on disk.
Every reachable object is re-hashed under SHA256 and tree, commit, and tag
references are rewritten accordingly. The command does not push to a
remote, does not modify the source, and is meant to run once per repo.
SHA256 hashes have no relation to the original SHA1 hashes beyond a
mapping the command can optionally emit.

## Quick Start

```bash
git-sync convert-sha256 \
  https://github.com/source-org/source-repo.git \
  /path/to/out.git
```

The target directory must not exist or must be empty. The result is a bare
repository with `extensions.objectformat = sha256` and a
`refs/notes/sha1-origin` ref recording each commit's pre-conversion SHA1.

Scope is fixed: every branch and every tag on the source is always
converted. Pass `--all-refs` to also include `refs/notes/*`,
`refs/pull/*`, and other custom namespaces; pair with
`--exclude-ref-prefix` to subtract specific namespaces (e.g.
`--exclude-ref-prefix refs/pull/` on GitHub mirrors).

For a private source, pass the token via the environment so it isn't
exposed in `ps`:

```bash
GITSYNC_SOURCE_TOKEN=ghp_xxx git-sync convert-sha256 \
  https://github.com/source-org/private-repo.git \
  /path/to/out.git
```

## What It Does

1. Probes the source via smart HTTP and lists every in-scope ref.
2. Fetches a single self-contained pack via `upload-pack` into a
   temporary on-disk SHA1 bare repo (cleaned up at the end unless
   `--keep-source-objects` is passed).
3. Discovers every reachable object — walking trees, commits, and tags
   — and records each one's SHA1 and object type. Submodule gitlinks
   are checked here; unresolvable ones fail-fast before any output is
   written.
4. Initializes the target as a bare SHA256 repository
   (`git init --object-format=sha256` equivalent).
5. Translates every reachable object in topological order via memoized
   DFS:
   - **Blobs**: re-hashed under SHA256; content unchanged.
   - **Trees**: each entry's hash translated.
   - **Commits**: `tree` and `parent` hashes translated; GPG signatures
     and `mergetag` headers dropped; in-scope SHA1 references in the
     message are translated first and then substituted.
   - **Tags**: target hash translated; signatures dropped; message
     hashes rewritten the same way.
6. Writes refs at the translated tip hashes; repoints HEAD to the
   source's symbolic HEAD; builds `refs/notes/sha1-origin` (unless
   `--no-origin-notes`); emits the `--write-mapping` TSV (if requested).

## Side Outputs

The conversion deliberately decouples SHA1 from SHA256 — two runs of
this tool against the same source produce SHA256 hashes that share
nothing with the originals. Three on-ramps help bridge the gap.

### Inline message rewriting (default on)

Commit and tag messages are scanned for 7-to-40-character hex runs.
When a run uniquely matches a commit or tag SHA1 in the reachable set,
it is replaced with the full SHA256 hex:

```
Reverts: a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2a3b4c5d6e7f8a9b0    →    full SHA256
Cherry-picked from a1b2c3d                            →    full SHA256
```

Two properties make this robust:

- **Uniqueness is decided against the reachable set, not the in-flight
  mapping.** The discovery pass enumerates every reachable SHA1 before
  any encoding starts, so abbreviated prefixes get the same verdict
  regardless of how far the translation has progressed. Ambiguous
  prefixes are left unrewritten and reported (warning on stderr +
  `--json`'s `ambiguousMessageRefs`); look them up in the mapping file.
- **Cross-branch references resolve.** Each in-scope SHA1 mentioned in
  a message is added as a dependency edge in the translation DFS, so
  the referenced commit is translated before the referencing commit is
  encoded. A cherry-pick from a sibling branch resolves just as
  reliably as a revert of an ancestor.

False positives are essentially impossible: a run is substituted only
if its prefix uniquely matches a commit or tag in scope. Blob and tree
hashes are excluded from the match set. Disable with
`--no-rewrite-messages` if you prefer untouched messages.

### Origin notes ref (default on)

`refs/notes/sha1-origin` holds, for each translated commit, the
pre-conversion SHA1 keyed by the new SHA256:

```bash
git -C /path/to/out.git notes --ref=sha1-origin show <sha256>
# prints the original SHA1

git -C /path/to/out.git log --notes=sha1-origin
# shows the original SHA1 below each commit's body
```

Notes attach meaningfully only to commits; blobs, trees, and tags are
not represented. Disable with `--no-origin-notes`.

### Sidecar mapping file (opt in via `--write-mapping`)

`--write-mapping <path>` emits a TSV with one line per translated
object, sorted by SHA1:

```
# sha1   sha256
00027b675386b21c4ca05316145671fb7034d251   d80415fa21bebb...
000bb155604d06f1c48fc7feb4b025d991ef3366   a23cf98db5abfa...
...
```

Useful for bulk rewriting external systems: feed the file to a script
that walks Jira tickets, PR bodies, deploy manifests, or any other
system that holds frozen SHA1 references.

## Flags

```
--source-url                       source repository URL
--source-token                     source password/token (prefer env)
--source-username                  source basic auth username (default git)
--source-bearer-token              source bearer token
--source-insecure-skip-tls-verify  skip TLS verification (testing only)
--source-follow-info-refs-redirect follow /info/refs cross-host redirects
--target-dir                       SHA256 bare repo directory (must be empty)

--all-refs                         also include refs/* outside heads/tags
                                   (notes, pulls, custom namespaces)
--exclude-ref-prefix               subtract refs by prefix; repeatable

--protocol                         protocol mode (auto, v1, v2)
--write-mapping                    write SHA1 → SHA256 TSV to this path
--no-rewrite-messages              skip inline hash rewrites in messages
--no-origin-notes                  skip refs/notes/sha1-origin
--keep-source-objects              leave the temp SHA1 store on disk
--json                             machine-readable output
--verbose, -v                      verbose logging
```

There are no `--branch`, `--tags`, or `--map` flags: scope is fixed to
every branch and every tag on the source.

Environment fallbacks: `GITSYNC_SOURCE_TOKEN`, `GITSYNC_SOURCE_USERNAME`,
`GITSYNC_SOURCE_BEARER_TOKEN`, `GITSYNC_SOURCE_INSECURE_SKIP_TLS_VERIFY`,
`GITSYNC_SOURCE_FOLLOW_INFO_REFS_REDIRECT`, `GITSYNC_PROTOCOL`.

## Sharp Edges

**GPG signatures are stripped.** A signature is bytes signed over the
commit's pre-conversion content (including the SHA1 hashes in `tree`
and `parent` lines). After rewriting, the bytes no longer match the
signature, so verification would always fail; the command drops them
and prints a count. Signed annotated tags lose their signature the
same way. `mergetag` headers on merge commits — which embed a signed
tag with its own signature — are removed entirely, since the embedded
tag references original SHA1s and the signature was computed over
those original bytes.

**Submodule gitlinks must resolve in-repo.** Tree entries with mode
`160000` reference a commit in another repository, but a SHA1 hash
cannot be embedded in a SHA256 tree. The command fails-fast in the
discovery pass — before the target bare repo is initialized — naming
the offending tree, entry, and hash. Convert the submodule repository
first so its commit hashes are available in SHA256.

**Replace refs and source notes refs become detached.**
`refs/replace/<sha1>` encodes a SHA1 in the ref name, so the name
doesn't match under SHA256 and the replacement never triggers.
`refs/notes/*` trees from the source (copied under `--all-refs`)
encode the target object's hash as the entry name, so notes survive
as data but no longer attach to their original commits. Use the
tool's own `refs/notes/sha1-origin` for the inverse lookup.

## Operational Notes

**One-off, not incremental.** Each run produces a fresh SHA256 repo
from scratch — there is no "fetch the new SHA1 commits and append to
the existing SHA256 repo" mode. Realistic use: convert once, then
make the converted repo the new canonical store. Branch and tag
hashes are deterministic across runs against the same source state;
only `refs/notes/sha1-origin` differs because its wrapper commit
carries `time.Now()` as the committer timestamp.

**Loose-object storage.** Every translated object is written as a
loose file under `objects/<aa>/<rest>` — no pack file is produced.
Correct, but slow on filesystems that dislike millions of small files.
Run `git -C <target> gc --aggressive` afterwards to pack the converted
repo down to a single packfile.

**Memory linear in reachable object count.** Two `map[Hash]…`
structures stay live for the whole run: `reachable` (SHA1 → object
type, built by discovery) and `mapping` (SHA1 → SHA256, built by
translation). At cobra scale (~5k objects), kilobytes; at Linux kernel
scale (~16M objects), roughly 2 GB peak.

**Discovery adds a ~1.5× decode pass.** Every reachable object is
decoded twice: once in discovery (no encoding) and once in translation
(decode + encode). The cost buys consistent uniqueness verdicts for
message rewriting and submodule fail-fast.

**Abbreviated-prefix lookup is a linear scan.** Each abbreviated SHA1
in a message triggers an O(reachable) scan to check uniqueness. Fine
to ~100k commits; slower past that. A sorted-prefix index would make
it O(log N), an easy optimization if someone hits the wall.

## Verifying the Output

Standard git tooling works against the converted repo without
additional flags — the `extensions.objectformat` setting in the local
config is enough for git to switch hashing:

```bash
git -C /path/to/out.git fsck --full                     # zero errors expected
git -C /path/to/out.git config extensions.objectformat  # prints sha256
git -C /path/to/out.git log --oneline -5                # SHA256 hashes
git -C /path/to/out.git log --notes=sha1-origin -5      # with original SHA1
```

To use the result as a working repo:

```bash
git clone /path/to/out.git /path/to/checkout
```

To serve it from a host that accepts SHA256:

```bash
git -C /path/to/out.git push --mirror <new-remote-url>
```

## Implementation Notes

The pipeline runs in four phases (pack fetch → discovery → target init →
translation), with refs and side outputs written at the end. Submodule
errors surface in discovery, before the target repo is materialized.

Translation is a memoized recursive DFS. Tree, parent, tag-target, and
message-reference edges are all part of the DFS, so the mapping is
populated by the time any object's bytes are encoded. A defensive
`inProgress` set guards against cycles; real Git histories can't form
them (parent/tree/tag-target edges are a DAG, and SHA1 message-
reference cycles are cryptographically infeasible), but a trip into
the guard becomes a hard error rather than a stack overflow.

Loose object writing is done by hand rather than via go-git's
`SetEncodedObject`. The underlying `plumbing/format/objfile.Writer`
in `go-git/v6@v6.0.0-alpha.3` hardcodes SHA1 in its hasher, which
would put every translated object at a SHA1-derived path even though
the content references SHA256. A unit test recomputes `sha256` of
every loose object's decompressed content and compares against the
filename to prevent regression.

Done. The doc shrank from 314 to 266 lines, with 8 sections instead of the previous mix of overlapping content.

**What I cut:**
- The "External SHA1 references break silently" sharp-edge paragraph (it just pointed back to the section above).
- The "HEAD always resolves" sharp-edge paragraph (not a sharp edge — and it referenced behavior that no longer exists).
- The standalone "Ambiguous prefixes are reported" paragraph (merged into the design-notes bullet inside "Inline message rewriting").
- The duplicate cross-branch / discovery / uniqueness restatement inside "Implementation Notes" (now just points at the pipeline and the workaround, doesn't re-explain the design rationale).
- The trailing "The temp SHA1 store is on disk…" paragraph at the end of "What It Does" (covered by Operational Notes now).

**What I renamed:**
- "Handling External SHA1 References" → "Side Outputs" — the three sub-sections describe outputs the tool produces, framed positively.

**What's new:**
- "Operational Notes" section with five bullets covering all six items you wanted documented (the one-off and notes-determinism points are folded into the same bullet — they share the same root cause).
- "Loose-object storage" moved here from "Sharp Edges" since it's an operational characteristic, not a correctness issue.

`go vet ./...` is unaffected (docs only); no code changed.

13	// progressReporter renders live per-side throughput to a writer (typically
14	// os.Stderr) by sampling the statsCollector's atomic byte counters on a
15	// fixed interval.

18	// above (used for in-place sideband progress like "source: Compressing
19	// objects: 89%") and the throughput ticker below. Each redraw uses

21	// place, so '\r'-terminated sideband updates from go-git read as a
22	// single updating row instead of scrolling line by line.
23	//
24	// On every render we also push the current per-side byte total into a
25	// short ring buffer (samples) so the displayed rate reflects recent
26	// throughput rather than a session-wide average — the latter
27	// undercounts the actual transfer rate because the divisor includes
28	// auth and ref-listing time when no pack data is flowing.
29	type progressReporter struct {
30		out      io.Writer
31		stats    *statsCollector
32		interval time.Duration
33		start    time.Time
34	
35		stopOnce sync.Once
36		stop     chan struct{}
37		done     chan struct{}
38

40		rowsDrawn int                    // rows currently occupying the live region
41		lastLine  string                 // last progress line, kept so setTransient can redraw without re-sampling
42		transient string                 // current sideband-progress line, "" when none
43		samples   map[string]*sampleRing // per-side sliding window of recent (time, bytes) snapshots
44	}
45	
46	func newProgressReporter(out io.Writer, stats *statsCollector, interval time.Duration) *progressReporter {
47		if interval <= 0 {
48			interval = 200 * time.Millisecond
49		}
50		return &progressReporter{
51			out:      out,
52			stats:    stats,
53			interval: interval,
54			start:    time.Now(),
55			stop:     make(chan struct{}),
56			done:     make(chan struct{}),
57			samples:  map[string]*sampleRing{},
58		}
59	}

61	// run drives the ticker until stop() is called. Safe to call once.
62	func (p *progressReporter) run() {
63		defer close(p.done)
64		ticker := time.NewTicker(p.interval)
65		defer ticker.Stop()
66		for {
67			select {
68			case <-p.stop:
69				return
70			case <-ticker.C:
71				p.render(false)
72			}
73		}
74	}

77	// J erases from the cursor to the end of the screen (covers both rows
78	// when the transient is shown); %dA moves the cursor up that many
79	// lines. Both are widely supported.
80	const clearDown = "\x1b[J"
81	
82	// cursorUpToTopLocked positions the cursor at column 0 of the top row
83	// of the currently-drawn live region. Always emits '\r' so the first
84	// render (rowsDrawn=0) still starts at column 0 instead of writing in
85	// the middle of whatever line the cursor was last on. Caller must hold
86	// p.mu.
87	func (p *progressReporter) cursorUpToTopLocked() {
88		if p.rowsDrawn > 1 {
89			fmt.Fprintf(p.out, "\x1b[%dA", p.rowsDrawn-1)
90		}
91		fmt.Fprint(p.out, "\r")
92	}

95	// row, erases everything below, then writes transient (if any) followed
96	// by the throughput line. Cursor is left at the end of the throughput
97	// row so subsequent ticker frames see rowsDrawn=region-height.
98	func (p *progressReporter) drawLocked(line string) {
99		p.cursorUpToTopLocked()
100		fmt.Fprint(p.out, clearDown)
101	
102		rows := 0
103		if p.transient != "" {
104			fmt.Fprintln(p.out, p.transient)
105			rows++
106		}
107		if line != "" {

120	// notice) where the previously-shown transient progress is no longer

122	func (p *progressReporter) notify(msg string) {
123		p.mu.Lock()
124		defer p.mu.Unlock()
125		p.cursorUpToTopLocked()

159	func (p *progressReporter) render(final bool) {
160		sides := p.stats.liveSides()
161		if len(sides) == 0 && !final {
162			return
163		}
164		sort.Slice(sides, func(i, j int) bool { return sides[i].Label < sides[j].Label })
165		elapsed := time.Since(p.start)
166		now := time.Now()
167	
168		p.mu.Lock()
169		// Sample first so the rate calculation below sees the latest data
170		// point. We hold p.mu so concurrent terminate/render cannot race
171		// the per-label ring buffers.
172		instant := make(map[string]float64, len(sides))

184		for i, side := range sides {
185			if i > 0 {

206	const (
207		sideSeparator    = "  │  "
208		flowArrow        = " → "
209		doneMark         = " ✓"

219		sampleCapacity = 10

222	// sample is one (time, cumulative bytes) snapshot taken at render time.
223	type sample struct {
224		at    time.Time
225		bytes int64
226	}
227	
228	// sampleRing is a fixed-size circular buffer of recent samples used to
229	// derive an instantaneous transfer rate from differences between the
230	// oldest and newest sample currently in the window.
231	type sampleRing struct {
232		buf   [sampleCapacity]sample
233		head  int // next write index
234		count int // valid entries (capped at sampleCapacity)
235	}
236	
237	func (r *sampleRing) push(s sample) {
238		r.buf[r.head] = s
239		r.head = (r.head + 1) % sampleCapacity
240		if r.count < sampleCapacity {
241			r.count++
242		}
243	}
244	
245	// instantRate returns observed bytes/second over the samples currently
246	// in the ring. Returns 0 when fewer than two samples exist, when the
247	// elapsed time between oldest and newest is too small to be meaningful,
248	// or when no bytes were transferred in the window (treat as idle so the
249	// formatter falls back to the active-window average).
250	func (r *sampleRing) instantRate() float64 {
251		if r.count < 2 {
252			return 0
253		}
254		newestIdx := (r.head - 1 + sampleCapacity) % sampleCapacity
255		oldestIdx := 0
256		if r.count == sampleCapacity {
257			oldestIdx = r.head // ring full — head currently sits on the oldest
258		}
259		newest := r.buf[newestIdx]
260		oldest := r.buf[oldestIdx]
261		dur := newest.at.Sub(oldest.at)
262		if dur < 50*time.Millisecond {
263			return 0
264		}
265		delta := newest.bytes - oldest.bytes
266		if delta <= 0 {
267			return 0
268		}
269		return float64(delta) / dur.Seconds()
270	}
271	
272	// formatSide renders a single side as host + bytes + rate, with a flow
273	// arrow positioned to indicate direction: source on the left of its
274	// counter, target on the right of its counter. Sides with neither label
275	// fall back to "name: bytes @ rate".
276	//
277	// While bytes are flowing (instantBytesPerSec > 0 and the side has not
278	// gone idle) the displayed rate uses the recent sliding-window number
279	// so it tracks the actual wire throughput. Once the side goes idle (or
280	// on the final render) we switch to the active-window average for a
281	// stable post-transfer headline. forceDone and idle gaps >idleThreshold
282	// append a "✓" marker.
283	func formatSide(side SideBytes, fallbackDur time.Duration, instantBytesPerSec float64, forceDone bool) string {
284		name := side.Display
285		if name == "" {
286			name = side.Label
287		}
288		name = truncateHost(name, maxHostnameWidth)
289	
290		done := side.Bytes > 0 && (forceDone || time.Duration(side.IdleNanos) >= idleThreshold)
291	
292		var rateText string
293		if !done && instantBytesPerSec > 0 {
294			rateText = formatBytes(int64(instantBytesPerSec)) + "/s"
295		} else {
296			rateDur := fallbackDur
297			if side.ActiveNanos > 0 {
298				rateDur = time.Duration(side.ActiveNanos)
299			}
300			rateText = formatRate(side.Bytes, rateDur)
301		}
302	
303		rate := formatBytes(side.Bytes) + " @ " + rateText
304		if done {
305			rate += doneMark
306		}
307	
308		switch side.Label {
309		case "source":
310			return name + flowArrow + rate
311		case "target":
312			return rate + flowArrow + name
313		default:
314			return name + ": " + rate
315		}
316	}
317	
318	// truncateHost shortens long hostnames while keeping the apex domain
319	// visible. Returns the original string if it already fits within width,
320	// otherwise preserves the trailing two dotted labels (e.g. "cloudflare.net")
321	// and spends the remaining budget on a prefix from the leading subdomain.
322	// Falls back to right-truncation when the apex alone doesn't fit.
323	func truncateHost(host string, width int) string {
324		if width <= 0 || len(host) <= width {
325			return host
326		}
327		labels := strings.Split(host, ".")
328		if len(labels) >= 2 {
329			apex := labels[len(labels)-2] + "." + labels[len(labels)-1]
330			if len(apex)+2 <= width { // room for at least one prefix char + ellipsis
331				prefixBudget := width - 1 - len(apex)
332				return host[:prefixBudget] + "…" + apex
333			}
334		}
335		if width <= 1 {
336			return "…"
337		}
338		return host[:width-1] + "…"
339	}
340	
341	// sessionStderr is an io.Writer that hands writes to the live progress
342	// reporter when one is attached to the syncSession, so verbose slog
343	// lines and server-side sideband progress ("Resolving deltas …") land
344	// above the in-place ticker frame instead of clobbering it. Falls back
345	// to os.Stderr when no reporter is active.
346	//
347	// Partial-line writes are buffered until a '\n' or '\r' terminator
348	// arrives. This matters for prefixedLineWriter, which writes a logical
349	// line in two calls — first the prefix ("source: "), then the content
350	// with terminator — and would otherwise produce two separate notify
351	// frames split mid-line. Use as a pointer (the buffer is stateful).
352	type sessionStderr struct {
353		s   *syncSession
354		buf strings.Builder
355	}
356	
357	func (w *sessionStderr) Write(b []byte) (int, error) {
358		if w.s == nil || w.s.progress == nil {
359			n, err := os.Stderr.Write(b)
360			if err != nil {
361				return n, fmt.Errorf("stderr write: %w", err)
362			}
363			return n, nil
364		}
365		s := string(b)
366		for s != "" {
367			i := strings.IndexAny(s, "\r\n")
368			if i < 0 {
369				w.buf.WriteString(s)
370				break
371			}
372			w.buf.WriteString(s[:i])
373			line := w.buf.String()
374			w.buf.Reset()
375			// '\r' marks an in-place sideband update (git's
376			// "Compressing 89%\r" → "Compressing 90%\r" pattern); '\n'
377			// marks a permanent line that scrolls. Route accordingly so
378			// percentage updates rewrite a single transient row instead
379			// of filling the scrollback.
380			if line != "" {
381				if s[i] == '\r' {
382					w.s.progress.setTransient(line)
383				} else {
384					w.s.progress.notify(line)
385				}
386			}
387			s = s[i+1:]
388		}
389		return len(b), nil
390	}
391	
392	// stderrIsTTY reports whether stderr is attached to a terminal. The
393	// progress ticker is suppressed otherwise because '\r' updates only make
394	// sense on a TTY and would otherwise spam log files.
395	func stderrIsTTY() bool {
396		fi, err := os.Stderr.Stat()
397		if err != nil {
398			return false
399		}
400		return (fi.Mode() & os.ModeCharDevice) != 0
401	}
402	
403	// formatBytes renders byte counts in IEC-ish human units (binary base).
404	func formatBytes(n int64) string {
405		const unit = 1024
406		if n < unit {
407			return fmt.Sprintf("%d B", n)
408		}
409		div, exp := int64(unit), 0
410		for x := n / unit; x >= unit; x /= unit {
411			div *= unit
412			exp++
413		}
414		value := float64(n) / float64(div)
415		suffix := []string{"KB", "MB", "GB", "TB", "PB"}[exp]
416		if value >= 100 {
417			return fmt.Sprintf("%.0f %s", value, suffix)
418		}
419		if value >= 10 {
420			return fmt.Sprintf("%.1f %s", value, suffix)
421		}
422		return fmt.Sprintf("%.2f %s", value, suffix)
423	}
424	
425	// formatRate renders a bytes/second average over the supplied duration.
426	// Returns "0 B/s" until the duration is large enough to be meaningful,
427	// avoiding misleadingly large rates from sub-millisecond samples.
428	func formatRate(bytes int64, dur time.Duration) string {
429		if dur < 50*time.Millisecond || bytes <= 0 {
430			return "0 B/s"
431		}
432		rate := float64(bytes) / dur.Seconds()
433		return formatBytes(int64(rate)) + "/s"
434	}
435

406		// inProgress detects cycles in the translation DFS. Real Git

409		// cryptographically infeasible), but a defensive guard turns

413		// for use by writeOriginNotes. We track separately rather than walking

435		if !ok {
436			return nil, fmt.Errorf("target storage is not filesystem-backed (%T)", dst)
437		}
438		if reachable == nil {
439			reachable = make(map[plumbing.Hash]plumbing.ObjectType)
440		}
441		return &translator{
442			src:                  srcFS,
443			dst:                  dstFS,
444			objectsDir:           filepath.Join(targetDir, "objects"),
445			reachable:            reachable,
446			mapping:              make(map[plumbing.Hash]plumbing.Hash),

content.text:
Done. Summary:

**CLI (`cmd/git-sync/convert_sha256.go`):**
- New `--progress` flag: "show live per-phase object counts on stderr (TTY only)".

**Library (`cmd/git-sync/internal/sha256convert/sha256convert.go`):**
- New `Request.Progress bool` field.
- `discoverReachable` now takes an optional `*atomic.Int64` progress counter; increments it once per visited object (nil disables the counter, used by all existing callers that don't want progress).
- Translator's int counters (`Blobs`, `Trees`, `Commits`, `Tags`) replaced with `atomic.Int64` fields (`blobs`, `trees`, `commitsCount`, `tags`). A `snapshotCounts()` method copies them into the existing `Counts` struct for the Result. This was necessary so the ticker goroutine can read counts without racing against the translation loop.
- New `startProgressTick(out, render) -> stopFn` helper: spawns a goroutine that rewrites a single line on `out` every 500 ms using `\r\x1b[K`. `stopFn` halts the goroutine, emits one final line, and terminates with a newline so subsequent output starts cleanly.
- New `isTTY(io.Writer) bool` helper. `--progress` is silently suppressed when stderr is not a terminal (pipes, log files), keeping `\r`-style control characters out of log output.
- `Run()` starts a ticker around the discovery phase ("discovered N objects") and the translation phase ("translated X blobs, Y trees, Z commits, W tags") when `Progress` is set and `out` is a TTY.

**Tests (`cmd/git-sync/internal/sha256convert/sha256convert_test.go`):**
- All `discoverReachable` callers updated to pass `nil` for the progress counter.
- `TestTranslator` updated to read counts via `tr.snapshotCounts()` and `tr.blobs.Load()` instead of the now-removed `tr.counts` field.
- All tests pass with `go test -race`.

**Docs (`docs/convert-sha256.md`):**

Real-world behavior: on a TTY, you'd see something like:
```
fetching 37 ref(s) from https://github.com/spf13/cobra.git ...
discovering reachable objects ...
  discovered 4567 objects
translating objects to sha256 ...
  translated 1867 blobs, 1601 trees, 1117 commits, 1 tags
sha256 bare repo: /tmp/...
```
Each "  discovered" / "  translated" line updates in place every 500 ms.
how can I test it?

Easiest way is just to run it interactively (no pipes/redirects), so stderr stays a TTY:

```sh
go build -o ./git-sync ./cmd/git-sync
rm -rf /tmp/cobra-progress
./git-sync convert-sha256 --progress \
  https://github.com/spf13/cobra.git \
  /tmp/cobra-progress
```

You should see two lines updating in place every 500 ms:

```
discovering reachable objects ...
  discovered 4567 objects
translating objects to sha256 ...
  translated 1867 blobs, 1601 trees, 1117 commits, 1 tags
```

The `discovered N objects` line replaces itself until discovery finishes, then prints its final value and a newline; same shape for translation.

**If you want to verify the TTY suppression works**, pipe stderr through `cat`:

```sh
./git-sync convert-sha256 --progress \
  https://github.com/spf13/cobra.git \
  /tmp/cobra-progress 2>&1 | cat
```

That should print *nothing* from the ticker — just the regular header/footer lines. The `\r\x1b[K` updates only fire when `os.Stderr` is a real terminal.

**If cobra finishes too fast to see the ticker tick more than once**, use a larger repo to give discovery/translation enough time:

```sh
rm -rf /tmp/k8s-sha256
./git-sync convert-sha256 --progress \
  https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes.git \
  /tmp/k8s-sha256
```

That has ~140k commits and millions of objects — the fetch dominates, but discovery and translation both take long enough that you'll see counters climbing for several seconds.

**Race-detector run** (worth doing once to confirm the atomic counters are correct in a live session):

```sh
go run -race ./cmd/git-sync convert-sha256 --progress \
  https://github.com/spf13/cobra.git \
  /tmp/cobra-progress
```

If there's any race between the ticker's reads and the translator's writes, this would catch it. (I already ran `go test -race` and it's clean, but a live race-detector run is a good extra check.)
how can I test the output?
no I mean fsck or other commands I shoudl run on the output to verity

Right, that's in "Verifying the Output" in the docs. The minimum set:

```sh
DST=/tmp/cobra-sha256

# 1. Integrity: no dangling objects, no malformed trees/commits.
git -C "$DST" fsck --full

# 2. Confirm SHA256 mode is actually set.
git -C "$DST" config extensions.objectformat       # → sha256
git -C "$DST" config core.repositoryformatversion  # → 1

# 3. Confirm HEAD resolves and you can walk history.
git -C "$DST" rev-parse HEAD
git -C "$DST" log --oneline -5

# 4. Confirm refs all resolve (no truncation, branches+tags accounted for).
git -C "$DST" for-each-ref --format='%(objecttype) %(refname) %(objectname)' | head

# 5. Confirm annotated tags survived correctly.
git -C "$DST" for-each-ref --format='%(objecttype) %(refname)' refs/tags/ \
  | grep '^tag ' | head -1 | awk '{print $2}' \
  | xargs -I{} git -C "$DST" cat-file -p {}
```

`fsck --full` is the load-bearing check. If the converter wrote a malformed object, an invalid SHA256 path, or a tree entry that doesn't dereference, `fsck` finds it and errors with a non-zero exit code. Zero output + zero exit = the repo is internally consistent under SHA256.

For deeper verification of specific features:

```sh
# 6. Pick a commit, confirm its origin SHA1 is reachable via the notes ref.
SHA256=$(git -C "$DST" rev-parse HEAD)
git -C "$DST" notes --ref=sha1-origin show "$SHA256"

# 7. Find a commit with a rewritten message reference and visually inspect.
git -C "$DST" log --all --pretty=format:'%H %B' \
  | grep -B1 'reverts [0-9a-f]\{64\}' | head -20

# 8. Mapping file integrity (if you ran with --write-mapping):
head -3 /path/to/mapping.tsv
wc -l /path/to/mapping.tsv
# Count should equal sum of Counts.Blobs + Trees + Commits + Tags from output.

# 9. Cross-check: tip SHA1 from source repo → look up in mapping → matches refs in target.
SRC_TIP=$(git ls-remote https://github.com/spf13/cobra.git refs/heads/main | awk '{print $1}')
grep "^$SRC_TIP" /path/to/mapping.tsv
git -C "$DST" rev-parse refs/heads/main
# Mapping's right-hand column should equal the rev-parse output.
```

If you want a single yes/no "did this work" command, it's just:

```sh
git -C "$DST" fsck --full && echo OK
```

That's what the integration test does (`TestRun_GitHTTPBackend`) — `fsck --full` on the result, fail if there's any "error" or "bad sha" in the output. Anything else is gravy.

## Verifying the Output

Standard git tooling works against the converted repo without
additional flags — the `extensions.objectformat` setting in the local
config is enough for git to switch hashing:

```bash
git -C /path/to/out.git fsck --full                     # zero errors expected
git -C /path/to/out.git config extensions.objectformat  # prints sha256
git -C /path/to/out.git log --oneline -5                # SHA256 hashes
git -C /path/to/out.git log --notes=sha1-origin -5      # with original SHA1
```

To use the result as a working repo:

```bash
git clone /path/to/out.git /path/to/checkout
```

To serve it from a host that accepts SHA256:

```bash
git -C /path/to/out.git push --mirror <new-remote-url>
```
input.new_string:
## Verifying the Output

Pass `--check` and the command runs four sanity checks against the
converted repo at the end of the run, printing one line each:

```
verifying output ...
  ✓ config: extensions.objectformat = sha256
  ✓ HEAD: ffe9fff421b77f2dcc049a95b3b8ba7b9da8976dd61bcf35e9fe2d993babc470
  ✓ refs: 37 / 37 resolve to objects
  ✓ git fsck --full: clean
```

The checks are:

1. **config** — `extensions.objectformat = sha256` is present in
   `<target>/config`.
2. **HEAD** — resolves to a non-zero hash and that object exists in
   the store.
3. **refs** — every written ref (except `refs/notes/sha1-origin`,
   counted separately) resolves to an object in the store. The count
   matches `RefsConverted`.
4. **git fsck --full** — the external `git` binary runs a full
   integrity check. Skipped (and reported as such) when `git` isn't
   on `PATH`; the conversion still succeeds.

If any check fails the command exits non-zero. The full per-check
results are also in `--json`'s `checks` array. Without `--check` no
verification runs and the run completes as soon as the conversion
itself finishes.

You can also run the checks by hand on a converted repo, with or
without `--check`:

```bash
git -C /path/to/out.git fsck --full                     # zero errors expected
git -C /path/to/out.git config extensions.objectformat  # prints sha256
git -C /path/to/out.git log --oneline -5                # SHA256 hashes
git -C /path/to/out.git log --notes=sha1-origin -5      # with original SHA1
```

To use the result as a working repo:

```bash
git clone /path/to/out.git /path/to/checkout
```

To serve it from a host that accepts SHA256:

```bash
git -C /path/to/out.git push --mirror <new-remote-url>
```

If any check fails, the command exits non-zero with the offending detail; the full set of per-check outcomes is also in `--json`'s `checks` array.

**What it does:**
- `config` — reads `<target>/config` and confirms `extensions.objectformat = sha256` is present.
- `HEAD` — resolves HEAD via go-git, confirms non-zero, looks up the object to ensure it exists in the store.
- `refs` — iterates every hash-reference except `refs/notes/sha1-origin`, confirms each resolves to an object, counts the resolves against `RefsConverted`.
- `git fsck --full` — shells out if `git` is on `PATH`; treats `error` / `bad sha` in output as failure. If git isn't installed, this step is recorded as `skipped (git not in PATH)` and still counts as OK.

**Tests:** the existing `TestRun_GitHTTPBackend` integration test now passes `Check: true` and asserts all four steps ran and passed. No new dependencies; uses `os/exec` for the fsck shell-out.

would it make sense to sign the last commit, meaning that the signer of that commit "signed" the whole translation?

Yes, the idea is mathematically sound — and it's exactly how `git verify-tag` already works. A signature over a commit's bytes transitively attests to everything reachable from it through parent edges, because every parent hash is in the signed bytes. So signing one tip really does cryptographically commit to that branch's entire history.

A few subtleties worth thinking through before we'd implement:

**1. Sign the tip commit, or sign an annotated tag pointing at the tip?**

Signing the tip commit means rewriting it with a signature header. That changes its hash, refs need to move to the new hash — fine, since a tip commit is a leaf (no cascade). But it's also weird: the signature would live on a commit whose `Author:` and `Committer:` lines are the original author/committer. `git verify-commit` would report it as signed-by-the-converter while showing committer = someone else. That's misleading.

Signing an annotated tag avoids the problem. The tag is a separate object, the tagger is explicitly the converter, and the tag's signature transitively covers the same history. We'd write, say, `refs/tags/converted/<branch>` as a signed tag pointing at each branch's tip. `git verify-tag refs/tags/converted/main` then attests the whole `main` history as the converter saw it.

I'd go with the tag option.

**2. Whose signature?**

The original authors' keys are gone — re-signing with them isn't possible without their cooperation. So the signature is necessarily the *converter's* attestation, not a restoration. Practically the converter is whoever runs the command: their GPG key, their SSH signing key, or an org-level "release bot" key.

The semantic shift is real: the converted repo's attestation chain is now "*X attests this is the conversion they produced from source Y at time T*", not "*the original author wrote this commit*". For internal mirrors, audit trails, or single-author repos that's a strict improvement over unsigned-everywhere. For broad public repos it's weaker than the original chain.

**3. How to actually sign without re-implementing crypto.**

Shell out to `git`. After our conversion finishes, run `git tag -s converted/<branch> <tip-sha256>` per branch — git picks up the user's existing `user.signingkey` and `gpg.format` config and produces a signed tag in the target repo. We don't have to know GPG vs SSH, RFC4880 packets, anything. The output is exactly what `git verify-tag` understands.

**4. What gets signed.**

One signed tag per branch covers each branch's reachable history. Anything not reachable from any branch tip — orphan refs under `--all-refs`, tag-only refs — would also need its own signed tag if you wanted full coverage. Simplest scope: one signed tag per `refs/heads/*` plus optionally one for `refs/notes/sha1-origin` so the mapping ref is attested too.

**5. Flag shape.**

`--sign` would do the work using whatever signing identity is configured in git, error out if none is configured. `--sign-key=<keyid>` could override.

**My take:** worth doing, scope it to "one signed annotated tag per branch via `git tag -s`, plus optionally `refs/notes/sha1-origin`". Default off. Cost is small (~80 LOC + a test). Value depends on whether your use case cares about audit-grade attestation — for an internal mirror that's load-bearing in compliance terms, very high; for a quick experimental conversion, basically zero.

Want me to implement it?
I think sounds great.

content.text: Now add a Signing subsection to "Side Outputs":

input.old_string:
### Sidecar mapping file (opt in via `--write-mapping`)

`--write-mapping <path>` emits a TSV with one line per translated
object, sorted by SHA1:

```
# sha1   sha256
00027b675386b21c4ca05316145671fb7034d251   d80415fa21bebb...
000bb155604d06f1c48fc7feb4b025d991ef3366   a23cf98db5abfa...
...
```

Useful for bulk rewriting external systems: feed the file to a script
that walks Jira tickets, PR bodies, deploy manifests, or any other
system that holds frozen SHA1 references.
input.new_string:
### Sidecar mapping file (opt in via `--write-mapping`)

`--write-mapping <path>` emits a TSV with one line per translated
object, sorted by SHA1:

```
# sha1   sha256
00027b675386b21c4ca05316145671fb7034d251   d80415fa21bebb...
000bb155604d06f1c48fc7feb4b025d991ef3366   a23cf98db5abfa...
...
```

Useful for bulk rewriting external systems: feed the file to a script
that walks Jira tickets, PR bodies, deploy manifests, or any other
system that holds frozen SHA1 references.

### Branch-tip attestation tags (opt in via `--sign`)

`--sign` shells out to `git tag -s converted/<branch> <tip>` for every
converted branch after the conversion completes. Each resulting
signed annotated tag is a cryptographic attestation by the converter
that the entire reachable history of that branch — every parent, tree,
and blob — is what the converter saw at conversion time. Anyone can
verify the chain afterwards with `git verify-tag refs/tags/converted/<branch>`.

The mechanism is the standard one: parent hashes are part of each
commit's bytes, so the tip's hash transitively commits to the whole
history. Signing the tip attests every ancestor.

Important nuance: the signature is by the *converter*, not by the
original authors (whose own signatures are necessarily lost — see
"GPG signatures are stripped" under Sharp Edges). The attestation
chain becomes "*X attests this is the conversion they produced*"
rather than "*the original authors wrote this commit*". For internal
mirrors or single-identity repos that's a strict improvement over
unsigned-everywhere; for broad public repos it is weaker than the
pre-conversion chain.

Signing uses the target repo's git signing config (`user.signingkey`,
`gpg.format`) by default — same as `git commit -S` or a normal
`git tag -s`. Override with `--sign-key <id>`, which is passed to
`git tag -s -u <id>`. SSH signing (`gpg.format = ssh`) and OpenPGP
both work because we shell out to `git`.

Requires the `git` binary on `PATH`. Signing failures (no key
configured, gpg/ssh-agent unavailable, etc.) abort the run after the
conversion has already completed — the target repo is left in a
valid converted state, just without the attestation tags. Re-run
`git tag -s converted/<branch> <tip>` manually once the signing
identity is set up.

content.text:
Done.

**Behavior:**
```sh
./git-sync convert-sha256 --sign \
  https://github.com/source-org/repo.git /tmp/out.git
```

After translation completes, the command shells out to `git tag -s converted/<branch> <tip>` for every converted branch, using whatever signing identity is in your git config (`user.signingkey`, `gpg.format` — SSH or OpenPGP, both work). The tag message reads:

```
SHA1 → SHA256 conversion attestation for refs/heads/main.

Source: <source-url>
Produced by git-sync convert-sha256.
```

Anyone can later verify the chain with `git verify-tag refs/tags/converted/main`. The signature attests the tip's bytes, which cryptographically commit (via parent hashes) to every reachable commit, tree, and blob in that branch.

**`--sign-key <id>`** overrides the default — passed to `git tag -s -u <id>`.

**Failure modes:** if `git` isn't on PATH or signing fails (no key configured, gpg/ssh-agent unavailable), the run aborts with a clear error *after* the conversion has finished. The target repo is left in a valid converted state, just without attestation tags. Re-run `git tag -s converted/<branch> <tip>` by hand once your signing config is sorted.

**Caveats** (also in the docs):
- The signature is by the *converter*, not by the original authors. Their signatures were dropped earlier in the conversion and can't be restored.
- The attestation chain becomes "X attests this is the conversion they produced," not "the original authors wrote this." Useful for internal mirrors and single-identity repos; weaker than the pre-conversion chain for broad public repos.

**Tests:** new `TestRun_GitHTTPBackend_Sign` integration test that spins up an ephemeral ed25519 SSH key, points `GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL` at a generated gitconfig with SSH signing, runs `convert-sha256 --sign`, and verifies the resulting `refs/tags/converted/main` is a signed annotated tag whose target equals the converted branch tip. Gated on `GITSYNC_E2E_SHA256_HTTP_BACKEND=1` and skipped if `ssh-keygen` isn't available.

**Docs:** new "Branch-tip attestation tags (opt in via `--sign`)" subsection under Side Outputs, plus the two new flags in the Flags block.
can you output a command example converting `entireio/cli` and addign `--sign` and a newly created temporary dir (with mktemp) as output?

```sh
OUT=$(mktemp -d) && ./git-sync convert-sha256 --sign \
  https://github.com/entireio/cli.git "$OUT"
```

`mktemp -d` creates an empty directory and prints its path; `$OUT` captures it for the convert-sha256 target. After the run, you can poke at the result:

```sh
git -C "$OUT" log --oneline | head
git -C "$OUT" verify-tag refs/tags/converted/main
```

If you also want the post-run sanity checks visible inline, add `--check`:

```sh
OUT=$(mktemp -d) && ./git-sync convert-sha256 --sign --check \
  https://github.com/entireio/cli.git "$OUT"
```

git-sync on  nodo/convert-sha256 [!] ❯ OUT=$(mktemp -d) && ./git-sync convert-sha256 --sign --check  https://github.com/entireio/cli.git "$OUT"
fetching 283 ref(s) from https://github.com/entireio/cli.git ...
discovering reachable objects ...
translating objects to sha256 ...
signing refs/tags/converted/1072-intermittent-stop-hook-timeout-after-30s-on-windows-with-concurrent-codexclaude-sessions ...
error: cannot run gpg: No such file or directory
error: gpg failed to sign the data:
(no gpg output)
error: unable to sign the tag
The tag message has been left in TAG_EDITMSG
error: convert-sha256: sign: git tag -s converted/1072-intermittent-stop-hook-timeout-after-30s-on-windows-with-concurrent-codexclaude-sessions: exit status 128

That's exactly the failure mode the docs describe — `git tag -s` needs a signing backend. By default it shells out to `gpg`, which you don't have installed. Easiest fix is to use SSH signing instead (no new tools to install):

```sh
git config --global gpg.format ssh
git config --global user.signingkey ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
```

(Substitute whichever public key file you actually have. If you don't have one yet: `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 -N ""`.)

Then re-run the conversion:

```sh
OUT=$(mktemp -d) && ./git-sync convert-sha256 --sign --check \
  https://github.com/entireio/cli.git "$OUT"
```

**Note:** the conversion *itself* succeeded on your last run — only the signing step failed. The target `$OUT` directory has a valid SHA256 repo, just without attestation tags. If you'd rather keep that one and only redo signing:

```sh
# Find every branch and create signed tags by hand, using whichever
# signing config you just set up.
git -C "$OUT" for-each-ref --format='%(refname:short) %(objectname)' refs/heads/ | \
while read branch tip; do
  git -C "$OUT" tag -s "converted/$branch" "$tip" \
    -m "SHA1 → SHA256 conversion attestation for refs/heads/$branch"
done
```

If you want gpg instead of SSH (e.g. you already use a GPG key elsewhere), install via `brew install gnupg` and the original command will work. SSH signing is the lower-effort path though, and `git verify-tag` works the same way against either.
do I have to upload the ssh key to github?

No. Signing is entirely local — `git tag -s` reads your SSH private key and writes a signature into the tag object. No network, no GitHub, no upload.

The three things uploading to GitHub *would* enable are:
- **GitHub's "Verified" badge** on signed tags/commits in their UI. Only matters if you push to GitHub and want the badge displayed.
- **`git verify-tag` working out of the box for *other people*** who clone your repo, *if* they fetch your allowed-signers file from a known place. Without that, others need to know which key to trust.
- **GitHub's own server-side signature verification** during pushes (rare, requires repo settings).

For your use case (converting `entireio/cli` to SHA256 into a local tempdir, with no hosting forge that even accepts SHA256 pushes today) none of those apply. The signature lives in the tag object on disk and is fully valid by itself.

Two small things to be aware of:

1. **Local verification needs an allowed-signers file** if you want to run `git verify-tag refs/tags/converted/main` yourself. Without it, git says "no principal matched". Setup is a one-liner:

   ```sh
   echo "test@example.com $(cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub)" > ~/.config/git/allowed_signers
   git config --global gpg.ssh.allowedSignersFile ~/.config/git/allowed_signers
   ```

   (Substitute the email you used as your git `user.email`. The format is `<email-or-principal> <ssh-key-line>`.)

2. **You can use your SSH auth key as the signing key**, or generate a separate one. Most people reuse their auth key — it's the same crypto primitive. The "should I upload to GitHub" decision is independent of either choice.

So: skip the upload, configure the allowed-signers file if you want to verify locally, and the rest works.

Worked end-to-end on a real repo — 22989 blobs, 6950 commits, 213 branches signed, fsck clean. Two small UX warts I noticed in your output that are worth tightening:

**1. The refs check line reads as off-by-default.**
```
✓ refs: 496 / 283 resolve to objects
```
The 283 was the original `refsWritten` count; the 213 attestation tags we just added pushed the live ref count to 496. The check passed (it's "≥", not "=") but the fraction looks broken. Easy fix: exclude `refs/tags/converted/*` from the count, or just report `refs: 283 / 283 resolve to objects` and report the attestation tags separately.

**2. The signed-tags summary line is enormous.**
```
signed 213 branch attestation tag(s): refs/tags/converted/1072-..., refs/tags/converted/20260116-..., ... (× 213)
```
For repos with many branches that's effectively a wall of text. Same shape of fix as we used for ambiguous prefixes: cap inline at 5 with a "...(N more)" suffix; full list still in `--json`'s `signedTags`.

Want me to make both tweaks?

how to I check the signature ?

Two steps: create an SSH allowed-signers file pointing at your public key, then run `git verify-tag`.

**Setup (one-time):**

```sh
mkdir -p ~/.config/git
echo "$(git config --global user.email) $(cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub)" \
  > ~/.config/git/allowed_signers
git config --global gpg.ssh.allowedSignersFile ~/.config/git/allowed_signers
```

The file format is just `<principal> <ssh-pubkey>`. You can have multiple lines for multiple keys/identities. The principal needs to match the `user.email` you signed with — `git config --global user.email` reads it back.

**Verify one tag:**

```sh
git -C "$OUT" verify-tag refs/tags/converted/main
```

Expected output:
```
Good "git" signature for you@example.com with ED25519 key SHA256:...
```

**Verify all 213 tags at once** (prints only failures, plus a summary):

```sh
fail=0
git -C "$OUT" for-each-ref --format='%(refname)' refs/tags/converted/ | while read tag; do
  if ! git -C "$OUT" verify-tag "$tag" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "BAD: $tag"
    fail=$((fail+1))
  fi
done
echo "verified all $(git -C "$OUT" for-each-ref refs/tags/converted/ | wc -l) tags"
```

**Inspect what's actually inside one signed tag** (raw view, useful for spot-checking):

```sh
git -C "$OUT" cat-file -p refs/tags/converted/main
```

You'll see the tag header (`object`, `type`, `tag`, `tagger`), the attestation message, and a `-----BEGIN SSH SIGNATURE-----` block at the bottom. That signature covers every byte above it, which transitively covers the entire reachable history via the `object <sha256>` line.

If a verify fails with "no principal matched" the allowed-signers email doesn't match the tagger's email. Check `git -C "$OUT" cat-file -p refs/tags/converted/main | head -5` to see the actual tagger email.
can you show me how to verity the signature of the last conversion? (/var/folders/n9/rqtwv3ms43q8jx9tmcynrmlm0000gn/T/tmp.S5PP3dNay8) of the main branch?

```sh
# 1. One-time: tell git which SSH key counts as "yours" for verification.
mkdir -p ~/.config/git
echo "$(git config --global user.email) $(cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub)" \
  > ~/.config/git/allowed_signers
git config --global gpg.ssh.allowedSignersFile ~/.config/git/allowed_signers

# 2. Verify.
git -C /var/folders/n9/rqtwv3ms43q8jx9tmcynrmlm0000gn/T/tmp.S5PP3dNay8 \
  verify-tag refs/tags/converted/main
```

Expected output (printed to stderr, exits 0 on success):

```
Good "git" signature for <your-email> with ED25519 key SHA256:<fingerprint>
```

If you'd rather skim the tag's contents first to see the message, tagger, and signature block:

```sh
git -C /var/folders/n9/rqtwv3ms43q8jx9tmcynrmlm0000gn/T/tmp.S5PP3dNay8 \
  cat-file -p refs/tags/converted/main
```

Substitute `~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub` with whichever public key you set as `user.signingkey` when configuring SSH signing. If your `user.email` differs from the email line in `~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub`, use the email — `verify-tag` matches against the principal in `allowed_signers`, which we just set to your git email.
this is the signature on the tag object right?

Yes — exactly. The signature lives inside the tag object itself (under `refs/tags/converted/main`), embedded as a `-----BEGIN SSH SIGNATURE-----` block at the end of the tag's body. The commit it points at — the SHA256 tip of `main` — is unchanged and unsigned.

The cryptographic chain works in the *other* direction: the tag's signed bytes include `object <sha256-of-main-tip>`, that commit's bytes include `tree <sha256>` and `parent <sha256>`, each parent's bytes include their own parents, etc. So tampering with any historical commit changes ancestor hashes, which changes the tip's hash, which changes the tag's `object` line, which invalidates the signature. The tag attests the whole history without modifying any of the history's bytes.

`git cat-file -p refs/tags/converted/main` shows the tag object structure plainly:

```
object 11a78c5d7ddc121d551d9bbf5e8fe7de2b329da77334cde2f2131ec44b1ef83d
type commit
tag converted/main
tagger Your Name <you@example.com> 1748000000 +0000

SHA1 → SHA256 conversion attestation for refs/heads/main.
Source: https://github.com/entireio/cli.git
Produced by git-sync convert-sha256.
-----BEGIN SSH SIGNATURE-----
...
-----END SSH SIGNATURE-----
```

The first 4 lines plus the body are what was signed; the `object` hash is the load-bearing one for attestation.

rqtwv3ms43q8jx9tmcynrmlm0000gn/T/tmp.S5PP3dNay8 ❯ git cat-file -p refs/tags/converted/main
object 11a78c5d7ddc121d551d9bbf5e8fe7de2b329da77334cde2f2131ec44b1ef83d
type commit
tag converted/main
tagger Andrea Nodari <and.nodari@gmail.com> 1779698132 +0200

SHA1 → SHA256 conversion attestation for refs/heads/main.

Source: https://github.com/entireio/cli.git
Produced by git-sync convert-sha256.
-----BEGIN SSH SIGNATURE-----
REDACTED
PowQqx6UX8bp0evB4AAAADZ2l0AAAAAAAAAAZzaGE1MTIAAABTAAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5
AAAAQLn2hdEdYiTF5MFug8L+mVD6fU/REDACTED
uk/lW8VIW/iErg9K46vA8=
-----END SSH SIGNATURE-----

That's exactly the expected layout. Five things worth pointing out:

1. **`object 11a78c5d...`** is the SHA256 tip of `refs/heads/main` in the converted repo. The signature commits to this hash, which transitively commits to the entire history through parent links.
2. **`tagger Andrea Nodari <and.nodari@gmail.com> 1779698132 +0200`** — `and.nodari@gmail.com` is the principal `verify-tag` will look for in `~/.config/git/allowed_signers`. Make sure that file has a line starting with exactly that email.
3. **Tag namespace pollution check** — note this tag lives at `refs/tags/converted/main`, not `refs/heads/main`. Your converted history's normal refs are untouched; the attestation lives in a parallel namespace. `git tag -l 'converted/*'` lists all 213.
4. **Signature size is tiny.** That ed25519 block is ~300 bytes regardless of repo size — the signature is over the tag's bytes, not the whole history. The "covers everything" property is structural, not size-driven.
5. **`git verify-tag refs/tags/converted/main`** is the one command that turns "I see a signature block" into "I trust that block was made by the holder of <key> for <these exact bytes>." Worth running once to confirm.

result.output:
[{"body":"## Summary

Fixes #63. Adopts git's own HTTP auth flow: try anonymous first, only consult the credential helper if the server returns 401.

- **Before**: `auth.Resolve` proactively ran `git credential fill` for every HTTPS endpoint with no explicit auth. On hosts the user had never authenticated against, git fell back to an interactive `Username:` / `Password:` prompt — turning git-sync into an interactive command. And for hosts where the helper *did* have credentials, we'd leak a token to public repos that didn't need one.
- **After**: anonymous request first. On 401, the new `CredentialHelper.Lookup` is consulted; on success we retry once, store the auth on the conn for follow-up `PostRPC` calls, and tell the helper to `approve` the credentials. The `GitCredentialHelper` implementation runs git with `GIT_TERMINAL_PROMPT=0`, so a misconfigured helper fails fast rather than blocking on a tty prompt.

### What about expired tokens?

If the helper's stored credentials have expired, the retry returns 401 (or 403 for token services like Cloudflare that surface "Invalid or expired token" as 403 instead of 401). In both cases we call `Helper.Reject` so the helper forgets the stale credentials and the next run starts clean.

The 403 path is deliberately scoped to the *retry result* only — never the initial response. A 403 on a first anonymous request usually means "this repo is forbidden to you" rather than "your credentials are bad", and we don't want to risk dropping good credentials for an archived/restricted repo.

## Test plan

- [x] Full `go test ./...` passes
- [x] `go test -race` on `internal/{gitproto,auth,syncer}`
- [x] `golangci-lint run` clean
- [x] New unit tests cover the full lifecycle: anonymous success skips the helper, 401 triggers Lookup + retry + Approve, retry-still-401 and retry-403 both trigger Reject, helper-with-no-credentials surfaces the original 401 cleanly, and explicit auth disables the helper fallback entirely
- [x] New `auth` tests cover `GitCredentialHelper.{Lookup,Approve,Reject}` and the `GIT_TERMINAL_PROMPT=0` env contract
- [x] Pre-existing end-to-end `TestRun_IntegrationUsesGitCredentialHelperFallback` still passes — exercises the 401-retry path against a real `httptest` basic-auth server
- [ ] Manual: reproduce the public-repo case from the issue (sync from `gitlab.baylibre.com` without credentials configured) and confirm no prompt

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Changes the smart-HTTP authentication flow to retry `/info/refs` with helper-supplied Basic auth on 401 and persist that auth for subsequent requests; mistakes here could cause auth failures or unintended retries. Risk is moderated by extensive new unit/integration tests and keeping explicit auth precedence unchanged.
> 
> **Overview**
> Switches HTTP auth resolution to **stop eagerly invoking** the git credential helper during `auth.Resolve`, preventing credential leakage to public repos and avoiding interactive prompts.
> 
> Adds a `CredentialHelper` hook to smart-HTTP connections so that when an anonymous `/info/refs` request returns **401**, git-sync performs a *single* credential-helper lookup, retries with Basic auth, then **persists the resolved auth** for the rest of the connection and signals `approve`/`reject` (including rejecting retry-403 as expired/invalid token). Also forces `git credential` calls to be non-interactive via `GIT_TERMINAL_PROMPT=0`.
> 
> Updates syncer wiring and tests accordingly, adding coverage for helper lookup/approve/reject behavior, non-HTTP endpoints, and ensuring explicit auth disables helper fallback.
> 
> <sup>Reviewed by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/bugbot) for commit 15b80ac519ca914fbd2e19208ce0b4fee0d8f88c. Configure [here](https://www.cursor.com/dashboard/bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->","number":65,"title":"auth: defer credential helper until 401, match git's behaviour"},{"body":"## Summary

- Diagnoses and fixes two independent bugs that broke materialized push against Cloudflare Artifacts (\`use of closed network connection\` mid-upload):
  1. **Stale keep-alive pool entry**: HTTPS info-refs and receive-pack ran ~tens of seconds apart in materialized push; the pooled TLS connection expired on Cloudflare's edge during that gap. Disabled keep-alives on git-sync's default transport (one extra TLS handshake per request, negligible against multi-MB transfers).
  2. **Mid-stream stall during delta selection**: go-git's encoder ran delta selection synchronously inside \`Encode()\`, producing no bytes for tens of seconds while the receive-pack body was open. CDN idle-write timeout fired. Fixed by using \`packfile.WithObjectSelector\` (landed in [go-git#2142](https://github.com/go-git/go-git/pull/2142)) to run selection up front and stream the write phase through \`io.Pipe\`.
- Adds two diagnostic env-var-gated tools that proved load-bearing during the investigation: \`GITSYNC_HTTP_TRACE=1\` (httptrace per-request lifecycle + outgoing-request dump with redacted Authorization).
- Adds in-place verbose-mode progress output for the materialized encode/write phases (\`selecting deltas, elapsed X\` → \`encoding pack: Y MB, elapsed Z\`).

## Commits

Each commit is scoped to a single change so the diagnostic story is rollback-friendly:

1. \`d8a071d9\` httptrace env var — debugging tool, kept
2. \`a3a96da\` disable HTTP keep-alives — fixes the stale-pool race
3. \`6f1f5944\` outgoing-request dump under trace — debugging tool, kept
4. \`f76e2308\` spool materialized push body — the original fix (temp-file workaround for the stall)
5. \`b22b2840\` pack-encode progress — UX for the long materialized phase
6. \`05be962e\` SpooledBody encapsulation + doc trims — post-review cleanup
7. \`8aa0dab7\` switch to go-git \`WithObjectSelector\` — replaces the temp-file spool with proper streaming, now that go-git#2142 is merged

Commits 4 and 7 tell the iteration: spool first (real workaround), then streaming (proper fix) once the upstream API was available.

## Verification

- Reproduced original failure against Cloudflare Artifacts target (\`use of closed network connection\` at ~7.9 KB sent).
- Confirmed stale-pool diagnosis via \`GITSYNC_HTTP_TRACE=1\` — saw \`reused=true wasIdle=true idle=13.001639584s\` immediately before the failure.
- Confirmed mid-stream stall via \`GITSYNC_FORCE_CHUNKED=1\` experiment (temporary toggle since reverted): spooled body + chunked still succeeded, ruling out chunked encoding as the cause and isolating the stall.
- Final commit (streaming via \`WithObjectSelector\`) confirmed working end-to-end against Cloudflare Artifacts.
- All existing tests pass; \`go vet\` and \`gofmt -l\` clean.

## Test plan
- [x] \`go test ./...\` passes
- [x] Manual: \`sync --all-refs --progress\` against Cloudflare Artifacts target succeeds
- [x] Manual: \`GITSYNC_HTTP_TRACE=1\` output confirms chunked encoding (no Content-Length on receive-pack POST) and no stale-pool reuse
- [x] Manual: verbose-mode progress shows \`selecting deltas\` → \`encoded pack\` phase transition

## Open items

- \`go.mod\` pins go-git to the merge commit of go-git#2142 (pseudo-version \`v6.0.0-alpha.4.0.20260521151600-590487407c38\`). Switch to a tagged release once go-git cuts \`v6.0.0-alpha.5\`.

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Changes smart-HTTP transport behavior (disables keep-alives) and rewrites materialized push pack encoding/streaming, which can affect connectivity and performance against various Git servers.
> 
> **Overview**
> Fixes materialized `receive-pack` uploads against CDN-fronted HTTP targets by upgrading `go-git` and changing `PushObjects` to precompute delta selection up front and then *stream* the encoder write phase through an `io.Pipe` using `packfile.WithObjectSelector`, avoiding mid-request idle stalls.
> 
> Hardens smart-HTTP behavior by cloning the default transport and **disabling keep-alives** to avoid reusing stale pooled TLS connections; adds optional env-gated (`GITSYNC_HTTP_TRACE`) `httptrace` logging plus outgoing request header dumps with `Authorization` redacted.
> 
> Adds verbose progress output for delta selection and pack encoding, and extends tests to assert chunked streaming for `PushObjects` and the updated `NewHTTPTransport` semantics.
> 
> <sup>Reviewed by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/bugbot) for commit 8aa0dab7f17c413efd27765509bfcb3fccad6196. Configure [here](https://www.cursor.com/dashboard/bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->","number":64,"title":"Fix materialized push against CDN-fronted HTTP targets (Cloudflare)"},{"body":"We're reassessing checkpoints v2 and working on a v1.1 with the parts that worked. We should be able to safely switch back to checkpoints v1 at this time (transcripts, commands etc. will fall back to v2 as long as the v2 ref is around).

We will keep the v2 checkpoint ref around for a bit while we work on fully migrating back to v1 for those checkpoints that were in v2-only mode.

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Low Risk**
> Low risk configuration cleanup limited to a single JSON settings file. Risk is limited to any tooling that still expects the removed `strategy_options.checkpoints_version` key.
> 
> **Overview**
> Simplifies `.entire/settings.json` by removing `strategy_options.checkpoints_version: "2"`, leaving only `enabled` and `telemetry`.

input.command:
gh pr create -R entireio/git-sync --title "convert-sha256: one-off SHA1 → SHA256 repo conversion" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

New `git-sync convert-sha256` subcommand. One-off conversion: fetches a pack over smart HTTP from a SHA1 source, walks every reachable object, and writes a fresh SHA256 bare repository where every tree/commit/tag reference is re-encoded under SHA256. All branches and tags are always converted (partial scope risks stranding cross-branch references in messages). Behavior, sharp edges, and operational characteristics documented in `docs/convert-sha256.md`.

## Design: two-pass topological DFS

The translation needs an authoritative "what's in scope" view *before* encoding starts, so abbreviated SHA1 prefixes in commit messages get the same uniqueness verdict regardless of how far translation has progressed, and so cross-branch message references (cherry-picks, reverts, etc.) can be added as dependency edges in the DFS:

1. **Discovery**: a non-encoding DFS from each desired ref tip walks every reachable object via tree entries, commit `tree`/`parent` links, and tag targets. Produces a `reachable map[SHA1]ObjectType` covering the full in-scope set. Submodule gitlinks pointing outside the repo error *here*, before the target bare repo is materialized — failing fast keeps half-converted state off disk.
2. **Translation**: memoized recursive DFS in topological order. Tree, parent, tag-target, *and message-reference* edges are all part of the DFS, so the SHA1 → SHA256 mapping contains every dependency by the time any object's bytes are encoded. Single forward sweep — no cascade re-encoding, no per-commit dirty bookkeeping.

Cycles in the combined dependency graph are cryptographically infeasible (forming a cycle via message references would require knowing each hash before computing it), so the topological order is always valid. A defensive `inProgress` guard turns unexpected graph shapes into a clear error rather than a stack overflow.

Loose objects are written by hand — `go-git@v6.0.0-alpha.3`'s `objfile.Writer` hardcodes SHA1 in its hasher, so the standard `SetEncodedObject` path would place every translated object at a SHA1-derived filename even on a SHA256 store. A unit test recomputes `sha256` of every loose object's decompressed content and compares against the filename to prevent regression.

## Signing (`--sign`)

After conversion, `--sign` shells out to `git tag -s converted/<branch> <tip>` for every converted branch. Each resulting signed annotated tag transitively attests the entire reachable history of that branch via the parent chain: tampering with any historical commit changes ancestor hashes, which changes the tip's hash, which changes the tag's `object` line, which invalidates the signature.

The signature is by the *converter*, not by the original authors — the originals' signatures are necessarily lost (they signed over pre-rewrite bytes). For internal mirrors and single-identity repos that's a strict improvement over unsigned-everywhere; for broad public repos it's weaker than the pre-conversion chain. SSH signing (`gpg.format = ssh`) and OpenPGP both work — we delegate to `git`, so whatever the user's git config picks is honored. `--sign-key <id>` overrides the default key.

## Example output

`--sign --check` against `entireio/cli` (283 refs, 6,950 commits, 55,289 objects):

```
$ OUT=$(mktemp -d) && ./git-sync convert-sha256 --sign --check https://github.com/entireio/cli.git "$OUT"
fetching 283 ref(s) from https://github.com/entireio/cli.git ...
discovering reachable objects ...
translating objects to sha256 ...
signing refs/tags/converted/1072-intermittent-stop-hook-timeout-... ...
signing refs/tags/converted/20260116-git-hook-absolute-path ...
signing refs/tags/converted/20260119-do-not-pin-go ...
... (one line per branch tip)
verifying output ...
  ✓ config: extensions.objectformat = sha256
  ✓ HEAD: 11a78c5d7ddc121d551d9bbf5e8fe7de2b329da77334cde2f2131ec44b1ef83d
  ✓ refs: 283 / 283 resolve to objects
  ✓ git fsck --full: clean
sha256 bare repo: /var/folders/.../tmp.S5PP3dNay8
source: https://github.com/entireio/cli.git (v2)
converted: 22989 blobs, 25350 trees, 6950 commits, 0 tags
refs written: 283
warning: stripped 4042 GPG signature(s); they no longer match the rewritten object content
rewrote 48 SHA1 hash reference(s) in commit/tag messages
origin notes ref: refs/notes/sha1-origin (use `git notes --ref=sha1-origin show <sha256>` to recover old SHA1)
signed 213 branch attestation tag(s): refs/tags/converted/1072-..., refs/tags/converted/20260116-..., refs/tags/converted/20260119-..., refs/tags/converted/20260128-..., refs/tags/converted/20260206-..., ... (208 more; full list in --json)
```

Inspecting one of the signed tags:

```
$ git -C "$OUT" cat-file -p refs/tags/converted/main
object 11a78c5d7ddc121d551d9bbf5e8fe7de2b329da77334cde2f2131ec44b1ef83d
type commit
tag converted/main
tagger Andrea Nodari <and.nodari@gmail.com> 1779698132 +0200

SHA1 → SHA256 conversion attestation for refs/heads/main.

Source: https://github.com/entireio/cli.git
Produced by git-sync convert-sha256.
-----BEGIN SSH SIGNATURE-----
REDACTED
PowQqx6UX8bp0evB4AAAADZ2l0AAAAAAAAAAZzaGE1MTIAAABTAAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5
AAAAQLn2hdEdYiTF5MFug8L+mVD6fU/REDACTED
uk/lW8VIW/iErg9K46vA8=
-----END SSH SIGNATURE-----
```

`git verify-tag refs/tags/converted/main` (against the user's allowed-signers file) then verifies the entire history.

## Other flags

- `--all-refs` / `--exclude-ref-prefix` — extend scope to notes/pulls/etc., or subtract
- `--write-mapping <path>` — emit SHA1 → SHA256 TSV for rewriting external systems (PR descriptions, deploy manifests, etc.)
- `--no-rewrite-messages` — skip inline rewriting of hash references in commit/tag messages
- `--no-origin-notes` — skip `refs/notes/sha1-origin` (per-commit reverse lookup of original SHA1)
- `--check` — post-conversion sanity steps (config, HEAD resolves, refs resolve, `git fsck --full`)
- `--progress` — live per-phase object counts (TTY only)
- `--sign-key <id>` — override `user.signingkey` for `--sign`

Full reference in [`docs/convert-sha256.md`](docs/convert-sha256.md).

## Test plan

- [x] `go test ./...` passes
- [x] `go test -race ./cmd/git-sync/internal/sha256convert/` clean (atomic counters used so the `--progress` ticker doesn't race against translation)
- [x] Unit tests cover the translator end-to-end: blobs/trees/commits/tags including signed commit + signed annotated tag, idempotency, on-disk `sha256(content)` invariant for every loose object, message rewriting (ancestor + cross-branch + ambiguous + skip), origin notes, mapping file, submodule fail-fast in discovery
- [x] Integration test against local `git-http-backend` (gated on `GITSYNC_E2E_SHA256_HTTP_BACKEND=1`): exercises pack fetch + discovery + translation + rewriting + notes + mapping + `--check` end-to-end, asserts `git fsck` clean
- [x] Signing integration test: spins up an ephemeral ed25519 SSH key via `ssh-keygen`, points `GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL` at a generated gitconfig with SSH signing, runs `convert-sha256 --sign`, asserts the resulting `refs/tags/converted/main` is a signed annotated tag targeting the converted branch tip
- [x] Smoke-tested manually against `spf13/cobra` (37 refs, ~4,500 objects) and `entireio/cli` (283 refs, ~55,000 objects); `git fsck --full` clean; `git verify-tag refs/tags/converted/main` passes against an SSH allowed-signers file

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)"

#MISE description="Lint go"

golangci-lint version

# In CI, we rely on golangci/golangci-lint-action@v9 defined in .github/workflows/lint.yml
# so only run this locally:
if [ "${CI:-}" = "true" ]; then
  echo "skipped because CI=true"
else
  if golangci-lint config verify >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    golangci-lint run --timeout=30m --fix ./...
    exit 0
  fi

  echo "golangci-lint config verification requires network; falling back to offline new-issues-only lint"

  if merge_base="$(git merge-base HEAD origin/main 2>/dev/null)"; then
    :
  else
    merge_base="$(git rev-parse HEAD~1)"
  fi

  golangci-lint run --no-config --new-from-rev="$merge_base" --timeout=30m ./...
fi

status="$(git status --porcelain=v1 go.mod go.sum 2>/dev/null)"

input.command:
git commit -m "Fix lint findings

golangci-lint v2.11.4 was unhappy across errcheck, exhaustive,
gocritic, inamedparam, ireturn, maintidx, noctx, perfsprint, revive,
and wrapcheck. Mostly mechanical:

- Test helpers (initSHA1, initSHA256, mustTranslator) collapse the
  '_ :=' patterns into t.Fatalf-on-error wrappers.
- exec.Command -> exec.CommandContext(ctx, ...); ctx threaded into
  runChecks and signBranchTips.
- refs.ForEach return value checked instead of discarded.
- if/else chain in runChecks rewritten as switch.
- ireturn for openSource/normalizeAuth annotated, since both return
  shared transport interfaces by design.
- maintidx on Run annotated; the function is a phase orchestrator,
  splitting it would obscure the pipeline.
- exhaustive switches on plumbing.ObjectType annotated; the
  unhandled cases (OFSDelta/REFDelta/AnyObject/InvalidObject) can't
  reach a resolved storer.
- Errors from io.ReadAll/MemoryObject.Reader/bufio.Flush/fmt.Fprintln/
  auth.Method.Authorizer wrapped with fmt.Errorf.
- Constant 'max' renamed to package-level 'previewMax' to stop
  shadowing the builtin in two places.
- Named parameter added to interface methods that lint flagged.
- Two fmt.Sprintf calls replaced with string concatenation."
